speaker: Unknown
en: ... idea of a PM makes no sense in the future.
zh: ... PM 的想法在未来毫无意义。

speaker: Unknown
en: The skill is more like being a CEO now, which is what are we building and why?
zh: 这种技能更像现在的 CEO，也就是我们在建设什么以及为什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of anxiety in the job market.
zh: 职场中有很多焦虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is going to radically reorient lots of people's careers and maybe including mine.
zh: AI 将从根本上重新导向许多人的职业，也许包括我的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I've noticed in some of the best organizations is the number one consumer of tokens is the CMO.
zh: 我注意到在一些最好的组织里，token 的第一大消耗者是 CMO。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't need to rely upon deputies and deputies and deputies to get actual work product.
zh: 他们不需要依靠副手以及副手的副手来获得实际的工作成果。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to hit on some contrarian takes that you have.
zh: 我想聊聊你持有的一些一些逆向观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your advice you don't actually want to be talking to customers.
zh: 你的建议是你实际上并不想与客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: I hate talking to customers.
zh: 我讨厌与客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: I refuse to allow colleagues of mine to talk to customers.
zh: 我拒绝允许我的同事去与客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have this idea of criticizing in public versus in private.
zh: 你有这种关于公开批评与私下批评的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: High performance machines don't have psychological safety.
zh: 高性能机器没有心理安全感。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're about winning.
zh: 它们是为了赢。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're uniquely great at helping companies build world-class teams.
zh: 你在帮助公司建立世界级团队方面非常出色。

speaker: Unknown
en: If a founder shows the ability early in his or her career to assess talent ruthlessly and accurately, here's how you can go very far with no other abilities whatsoever.
zh: 如果一位创始人在其职业生涯早期就表现出无情且准确地评估人才的能力，以下是你可以即使没有任何其他能力也能走得很远的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like it's never been harder to attract the best talent.
zh: 感觉现在吸引顶尖人才从未如此艰难。

speaker: Unknown
en: Really talented people.
zh: 真正有才华的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: When things are going well, they're not happy.
zh: 当事情进展顺利时，他们并不开心。

speaker: Unknown
en: The morale actually does go down when people are skating.
zh: 当人们在滑行时，士气实际上确实会下降。

speaker: Unknown
en: The single role for the CEO is offsetting that complacency.
zh: CEO 的唯一角色就是抵消那种自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: The better you're doing, the more the CEO should push.
zh: 你做得越好，CEO 就越应该推动。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Keith Rabois.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Keith Rabois。

speaker: Unknown
en: Keith's resume, both as an operator and investor is absurd.
zh: Keith 的简历，无论是作为从业者还是投资者，都荒谬得令人难以置信。

speaker: Unknown
en: He was an early investor in Stripe, Palantir, Airbnb, YouTube, DoorDash, Ramp, and dozens of other companies.
zh: 他是 Stripe、Palantir、Airbnb、YouTube、DoorDash、Ramp 和其他几十家公司的早期投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's part of the famous PayPal Mafia, where he was executive vice president of business development and policy.
zh: 他是著名的 PayPal 黑手党的一员，曾任业务发展和政策执行副总裁。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's also been chief operating officer at Square, VP of corporate development at LinkedIn.
zh: 他也曾担任 Square 的首席运营官，LinkedIn 的企业发展副总裁。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's also co-founded two companies, and he's currently managing director at Khosla Ventures.
zh: 他还联合创立了两家公司，目前是 Khosla Ventures 的董事总经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's safe to say that Keith is in the 99.9th percentile at identifying talent, building teams, and operating world-class companies.
zh: 可以说，Keith 在识别人才、建立团队和运营世界级公司方面处于 99.9% 的分位。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out lennysproductpass.com for an incredible set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在我们开始之前，别忘了去 lennysproductpass.com 看看，那里有专门提供给 Lenny's Newsletter 订阅者的惊人优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Keith Rabois.
zh: 有了这个，我为你请来 Keith Rabois。

speaker: Unknown
en: Keith, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.
zh: Keith，非常感谢你的到来，欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a pleasure to be with you.
zh: 很高兴和你在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, so when we were starting this recording, you told me you're doing this from an iPad, which I've never had.
zh: 好的，所以当我们开始录音时，你告诉我你正在用 iPad 做这件事，这是我从未有过的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you've shared a crazy fact that you haven't used a computer in years.
zh: 而且你分享了一个疯狂的事实，你已经好几年没用过电脑了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about what's going on there.
zh: 谈谈那里发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when I started working at Square, Jack Dorsey was running the company off an iPad.
zh: 所以当我在 Square 开始工作时，Jack Dorsey 正用 iPad 运营公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I immediately converted in September 2010 and haven't looked back.
zh: 所以我在 2010 年 9 月立即转变了，并且再也没有回头。

speaker: Unknown
en: I haven't touched a computer since September 2010.
zh: 自 2010 年 9 月以来，我再也没有碰过电脑。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything I do in my life is either done from my phone, my watch, or my iPad.
zh: 我生活中所做的一切要么是用手机、手表，要么是用 iPad 完成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's so interesting about this as you were talking is just there's this trend of engineers starting to code from their phone, at Boris Cherny on and Simon Willison, these two engineers that are 10X engineers, and they're just coding from their phone talking to AI.
zh: 关于这一点，最有趣的是，正如你所说，工程师开始从手机编写代码的趋势正在兴起，比如 Boris Cherny 和 Simon Willison，这两位是 10 倍工程师，他们只是在用手机跟 AI 对话来写代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I feel like you've been preparing for this for a long time.
zh: 我觉得你为此准备了很长时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm trying to be ahead of the curve.
zh: 我试图走在曲线前面。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jack's very good at being ahead of the curve.
zh: Jack 非常擅长走在曲线前面。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you just watch what Jack's doing and follow, you're in pretty good shape in technology.
zh: 如果你只是观察 Jack 在做什么并跟随，你在技术领域就会处于很好的状态。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just to understand the benefit, is it just avoid distractions?
zh: 为了理解这一点的好处，仅仅是为了避免干扰吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, partially distractions, partially just the flexibility.
zh: 是的，部分是为了避免干扰，部分是为了灵活性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Taking an iPad with you anywhere is just super easy.
zh: 随身携带一台 iPad 到任何地方都超级容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some laptops have improved since then, but the screen flexibility, angles, but just the weight.
zh: 一些笔记本电脑自那时起有所改进，但在屏幕灵活性、角度以及仅仅是重量方面。

speaker: Unknown
en: I carry my iPad with me everywhere.
zh: 我到哪儿都带着我的 iPad。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's nothing you can't perform unless maybe if you're doing heavy duty engineering, which obviously has not been my forte in life, although I may have to start.
zh: 没有什么是你不能执行的，除非也许你在做重型工程，这显然不是我人生中的强项，尽管我可能得开始了。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's no reason to use a more powerful, heavier weight, less flexible machine.
zh: 没有理由去使用一台更强大、更重、灵活性更差的机器。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: iPad Maxing, Keith Rabois.
zh: iPad Maxing，Keith Rabois。

speaker: Unknown
en: See if you got my Apple.
zh: 看看你有没有收到我的 Apple。

speaker: Unknown
en: As long as it's an Apple product, it works.
zh: 只要它是 Apple 产品，就能工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is so appropriate.
zh: 这真是太恰当了。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by our season's presenting sponsor, WorkOS.
zh: 本集由我们本季的展示赞助商 WorkOS 赞助播出。

en: What do OpenAI, Anthropic, Cursor, Vercel, Replit, Sierra, Clay, and hundreds of other winning companies all have in common?
zh: OpenAI、Anthropic、Cursor、Vercel、Replit、Sierra、Clay 和数百家其他获胜的公司有什么共同点？

speaker: Unknown
en: They are all powered by WorkOS.
zh: 它们都由 WorkOS 提供支持。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're building a product for the enterprise, you've felt the pain of integrating single sign-on, Skim, R-back, audit logs, and other features required by large companies.
zh: 如果你在为企业构建产品，你已经感受到了集成单点登录、Skim、R-back、审计日志以及大公司要求的其他功能的痛苦。

speaker: Unknown
en: Work OS turns those deal blockers into drop-in APIs with a modern developer platform built specifically for B2B SaaS.
zh: WorkOS 利用专门为 B2B SaaS 构建的现代化开发者平台，将这些交易障碍变成了现成的 API。

speaker: Unknown
en: Literally, every startup that I'm an investor in that starts to expand upmarket ends up working with WorkOS, and that's because they are the best.
zh: 说实话，我投资的每一家开始向上市场扩张的初创公司最终都会与 WorkOS 合作，因为他们是最好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you are a seed stage startup trying to land your first enterprise customer or a unicorn expanding globally, WorkOS is the fastest path to becoming enterprise-ready and unblocking growth.
zh: 无论你是试图拿下第一个企业客户的种子期初创公司，还是正在全球扩张的独角兽企业，WorkOS 都是成为企业就绪的最快途径，也是解除增长阻碍的途径。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's essentially Stripe for enterprise features.
zh: 它本质上就是企业功能的 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: Visit workos.com to get started or just hit up their Slack where they have actual engineers waiting to answer your questions.
zh: 访问 workos.com 开始使用，或者直接加入他们的 Slack，那里有真正的工程师在等着回答你的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: WorkOS allows you to build faster with delightful APIs, comprehensive docs, and a smooth developer experience.
zh: WorkOS 允许你通过令人愉悦的 API、全面的文档和流畅的开发者体验来更快地构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Go to workos.com to make your app enterprise ready today.
zh: 访问 workos.com 让你的应用今天就为企业就绪。

speaker: Unknown
en: As I was preparing for this chat, there's just so many directions we could have gone with this.
zh: 当我为这次聊天做准备时，我们可以聊的方向实在太多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You are so smart on so many things.
zh: 你在这么多事情上都太聪明了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to focus on something that I think you're uniquely great at, which is helping companies build world-class teams.
zh: 我想专注于一件我认为你独特地擅长的事情，那就是帮助公司建立世界级的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I want to start in particular with attracting the best talent.
zh: 我想特别从吸引最优秀的人才开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what's really interesting these days, from what I can tell, is there's just a lot of people that are struggling to find a job.
zh: 如今真正有趣的是，据我所知，有很多人正在挣扎着找工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's taking a lot longer to find a job.
zh: 找工作花费的时间更长了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just there's all these layoffs.
zh: 只是有所有这些裁员。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the other hand, it feels like it's never been harder to attract the best talent.
zh: 另一方面，感觉吸引最优秀的人才从来没有这么难过。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are so many amazing companies doing amazing things, so much money flying around, these hundred million dollar offers and things.
zh: 有太多令人惊叹的公司在做着令人惊叹的事情，到处都是钱，这些一亿美元的报价之类的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so from the companies that you are closest to that you see are best at attracting the best talent, what have they figured out?
zh: 那么，对于那些你最亲近的、你认为在吸引最优秀人才方面做得最好的公司，它们发现了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are they doing differently?
zh: 它们做有什么不同？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are some creative things they do?
zh: 它们做了一些什么有创意的事情？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, let's start with first principles.
zh: 好吧，让我们从第一性原理开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: The most important lesson I learned when I was working at Square from my board was Vinod Khosla was on my board, and he said, "The team you build is the company you build."
zh: 我在 Square 工作时从董事会那里学到的最重要的教训是 Vinod Khosla 在我的董事会里，他说，“你建立的团队就是你建立的公司。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that adage is the most important thing when you're creating a startup.
zh: 这个格言在创建初创公司时是最重要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: People get distracted with the market, with customers, with a product, with technology.
zh: 人们会被市场、客户、产品、技术所分散注意力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Ultimately, it's the team.
zh: 归根结底，是团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you have the right people, everything else will be easy and you have the wrong people, everything else is going to be difficult.
zh: 如果你的人对了，其他一切都会很容易，如果你的人错了，其他一切都会很困难。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, Vinod distilled it, but I actually learned this back on my PayPal days.
zh: 所以，Vinod 总结了这一点，但我实际上早在我的 PayPal 日子时就学到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So in the early 2000s, why was PayPal so successful?
zh: 那么在 2000 年代初，为什么 PayPal 会如此成功？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why were there subsequent generations of successful, interesting companies for 25 years now?
zh: 为什么在随后的 25 年里会有几代成功的、有趣的公司？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's because Peter Thiel and Max Levchin marshaled an incredible density of talent.
zh: 这是因为 Peter Thiel 和 Max Levchin 调动了令人难以置信的人才密度。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it allowed PayPal to succeed where possibly we wouldn't have.
zh: 所以这使得 PayPal 在我们可能无法成功的地方获得了成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: And these people went on with interesting ideas, ambition, and talent to build epic companies in all kinds of verticals.
zh: 这些人带着有趣的想法、雄心和才华，去各种垂直领域建立了史诗般的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: So from day one, day one of my technical career, technology career, I've always been focused on the importance of critical density talent, how do you identify, retain, and promote people with that talent?
zh: 所以从我的技术职业生涯、技术生涯的第一天起，我一直专注于关键人才密度的重要性，你如何识别、保留和提拔拥有这种才华的人？

speaker: Unknown
en: Back in the PayPal days, when I first started my career in technology, I actually truthfully was not very good at this.
zh: 回到 PayPal 的日子，当我刚开始我的技术职业生涯时，实际上说实话我并不太擅长这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Fortunately, Peter and Max were.
zh: 幸运的是，Peter 和 Max 擅长这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So Max basically hired all the technical talent in the organization.
zh: 所以 Max 基本上雇佣了组织里所有的技术人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter pretty much hired everybody else.
zh: Peter 几乎雇佣了其他所有人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They used their network primarily.
zh: 他们主要利用了他们的人脉网络。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it was very difficult to get a job at PayPal, unless you had a first degree or second degree connection to the engineering team or to Peter through Stanford.
zh: 所以很难在 PayPal 找到一份工作，除非你与工程团队有一层或二层关系，或者通过 Stanford 与 Peter 有关系。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is a different type of recruiting model.
zh: 这是一种不同类型的招聘模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: It works really well if you have a strong network.
zh: 如果你拥有强大的人脉网络，它运作得非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wouldn't recommend it for everybody, but if you have a network that has unique talent, there's no substitute.
zh: 我不会向所有人推荐它，但如果你的人脉网络拥有独特的人才，那是无可替代的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Interviews are not a great substitute regardless of how strong you are at interviewing.
zh: 无论你的面试能力有多强，面试都不是一个很好的替代品。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when I started my career, I was mediocre at let's say hiring people, probably 50/50.
zh: 但当我开始我的职业生涯时，我在比如说招聘人员方面很平庸，大概是五五开。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some good people, some mediocre people.
zh: 一些优秀的人，一些平庸的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: That doesn't allow you to scale a team with an unfair advantage.
zh: 这无法让你以一种不公平的优势来扩大团队规模。

speaker: Unknown
en: But what I learned to do is steal people from other people's organizations within PayPal very successfully.
zh: 但我学会做的是非常成功地从 PayPal 内部其他人的组织里挖人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I got feedback from David Sachs, who was the COO at the time that I wasn't going to get promoted again until I could demonstrate leverage, leadership leverage, which he had an equation for of one plus one has to equal three or more.
zh: 所以我收到了 David Sachs 的反馈，他当时的 COO，他说在我能够证明杠杆作用、领导力杠杆之前，我不能再被晋升了，他对此有一个公式，一加一必须等于三或更多。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for every incremental person you hire, you have to show that you produce disproportionate returns, non-linear returns.
zh: 所以对于你招聘的每一个增量人员，你必须展示你产生了不成比例的回报，非线性的回报。

speaker: Unknown
en: And because I wasn't hiring that well, I wasn't really succeeding at that leverage.
zh: 而且因为我招聘得不是很好，我真的没有在那个杠杆上取得成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when I went around, took the feedback into account, I said, "Okay, I really want to get promoted.
zh: 所以当我四处走动，把这些反馈纳入考虑时，我说，“好的，我真的想获得晋升。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do I do?"
zh: 我该做什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I found people within the organization that I felt had talent that were not being leveraged to the highest potential inhibition, and I recruited them to my team.
zh: 所以我在组织内部找到了一些我认为有才华但没有被发挥到最高潜力抑制的人，我把他们招募到了我的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was very successful.
zh: 那是非常成功的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I did get promoted actually fairly quickly because these people actually left to speed.
zh: 然后我确实很快就得到了晋升，因为这些人员确实留下了速度。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were able to run really fast and we produced a lot of really important results for the company.
zh: 他们能够跑得非常快，我们为公司产生了很多真正重要的成果。

speaker: Unknown
en: The lesson though that I took away was, well, that's great because what it showed to me is I actually could identify talent.
zh: 但我吸取的教训是，好吧，这太棒了，因为它向我展示了实际上我能够识别人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just couldn't identify strangers with talent.
zh: 我只是无法识别陌生人中的人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: So people that were in the building that I have lunch with or dinner with or go for a runaround the PayPal campus with, I was accurate at diagnosing their abilities.
zh: 所以对于在大楼里的人，我和他们一起吃午饭或晚饭，或者在 PayPal 校园里和他们一起跑步，我能准确地诊断他们的能力。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just couldn't do it in a 20-minute, 30-minute, 45-minute interview.
zh: 我只是在 20 分钟、30 分钟、45 分钟的面试中做不到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the first thing is just double down on people I know.
zh: 所以第一件事就是加倍押注在我认识的人身上。

speaker: Unknown
en: That doesn't scale perfectly, but learn to be excellent at, if I had context, assessing people's abilities, superpowers and weaknesses.
zh: 这不能完美地扩展，但要学着变得出色，如果我有背景的话，评估人们的能力、超能力和弱点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then over the next X years, tried to identify a different technique for identifying, assessing random people.
zh: 然后在接下来的 X 年里，试图找到一种不同的技术来识别、评估随机的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because ultimately if you're going to build an organization, ultimately if you're going to be a VC, you can't just invest in people that you already know.
zh: 因为最终如果你要建立一个组织，最终如果你要成为一名 VC，你不能只投资于你已经认识的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that took some years and required some training.
zh: 所以那花了一些年并且需要一些训练。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anyway, I think you can teach some of this to a founder, but one advantage a founder has that's going to thrive is if a founder shows the ability early in his or her career to assess talent ruthlessly and accurately, here's how you can go very far with no other abilities whatsoever.
zh: 无论如何，我认为你可以把其中一些教给创始人，但一个即将茁壮成长的创始人的一个优势是，如果创始人在其职业生涯早期就展示了无情且准确地评估人才的能力，这是你可以在没有任何其他能力的情况下走得非常远的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Hiring is like a muscle.
zh: 招聘就像一块肌肉。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to exercise it.
zh: 你需要锻炼它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to try, learn what worked, what didn't, what could you have known, what did you miss, why, and riff on that and try to get better at it.
zh: 你需要尝试，学习什么有效，什么无效，你能知道什么，你错过了什么，为什么，并即兴发挥并尝试做得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are tactics you can learn.
zh: 有一些你可以学习的策略。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the tactics work pretty well within the middle of a bell curve distribution of moving yourself 10, 20 degrees within that bell curve.
zh: 我认为这些策略在钟形曲线分布的中间部分，即在该钟形曲线内将自己移动 10、20 度方面效果相当好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think the tactics can really teach you how to identify, call it top 10 basis points, top 50 basis points of talent.
zh: 我不认为这些策略真的能教你如何识别，比如说前 10 个基点，前 50 个基点的人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: There you have to deviate from the norm.
zh: 在那里你必须偏离规范。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's actually true of most lessons in life.
zh: 我认为这实际上适用于生活中的大多数教训。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're going to be extraordinary in any skill, you can't follow a playbook.
zh: 如果你打算在任何技能上变得非凡，你就不能遵循剧本。

speaker: Unknown
en: Otherwise, by definition, lots of other people would be the top 10 basis points, but you can get a lot better by learning techniques.
zh: 否则，根据定义，很多其他人都会是前 10 个基点，但你可以通过学习技术变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, let's share a couple.
zh: 所以例如，让我们分享几个。

speaker: Unknown
en: One thing I think you can learn to do is be excellent at references.
zh: 我认为你可以学会做的一件事是擅长做背景调查。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't work for hiring people right out of college or something because the reference context is going to be a little off, but I think without getting better at interviewing and assessing, if you just learn to extract the right information from ruthless referencing.
zh: 它不适合雇佣刚毕业的大学生或类似情况，因为推荐人背景会有一点偏差，但我认为如果不去提高面试和评估，如果你只是学会从无情的背景调查中提取正确的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, ruthless referencing to me means Tony at DoorDash does 20 references on every single senior hire, 20.
zh: 所以例如，对我来说无情的背景调查意味着 DoorDash 的 Tony 对每一位高级招聘都做 20 个背景调查，20 个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow.
zh: 哇。

speaker: Unknown
en: I bet you he's pretty good and that he's been pretty accurate too.
zh: 我敢打赌他相当不错，而且他也相当准确。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think you can learn that.
zh: 所以我认为你可以学习那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a skill that's teachable, that being absolutely incredibly dedicated to your craft, you can just get better at.
zh: 这是一项可教的技能，即绝对难以置信地致力于你的手艺，你可以变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Back in the day, there's an investor at Greylock who's on my board at LinkedIn, David Sze, very successful investor, notable for both LinkedIn and Facebook investments.
zh: 回过头，Greylock 有一位投资者，他在 LinkedIn 的董事会里，David Sze，非常成功的投资者，以对 LinkedIn 和 Facebook 的投资而闻名。

speaker: Unknown
en: He used to teach at Greylock, you couldn't stop referencing a founder until you hit a negative reference.
zh: 他过去在 Greylock 教导，你不能停止推荐一位创始人，直到你遇到一个负面的推荐。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you would know you had exhausted the reference when you finally hit a negative reference.
zh: 所以当你最终遇到一个负面推荐时，你会知道你已经穷尽了推荐。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think there are tactics there in muscle building.
zh: 所以我认为那里有一些策略和肌肉构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you get the right information from the right people?
zh: 你如何从对的人那里获得正确的信息？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you frame the questions, et cetera?
zh: 你要如何构建这些问题，等等？

speaker: Unknown
en: That will lead you in the right direction.
zh: 那会引你走向正确的方向。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, you have to be careful, let's say, I'll give you an example where this can go wrong.
zh: 现在，你必须小心，让我给你一个可能会出错的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've been a longtime investor from the seed round of a company called Faire, founded by two of my colleagues at Square, Max Rhodes and Jeff Colvison worked for me at Square and then the two other co-founders also worked at Square.
zh: 我是这家名叫 Faire 的公司的长期投资者，从种子轮开始，由我在 Square 的两位同事 Max Rhodes 和 Jeff Colvison 创立，Jeff Colvison 曾在 Square 为我工作，另外两位联合创始人也在 Square 工作过。

speaker: Unknown
en: When people were reference checking Max, often most VCs asked the wrong question, which was, "Was Max a good employee?"
zh: 当人们做 Max 的背景调查时，大多数风险投资人经常问错问题，那个问题是，“Max 是一名好员工吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: The answer to that is very mixed.
zh: 这个答案是很复杂的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so some venture capitalists, including some very good ones, were nervous about investing in Faire.
zh: 因此一些风险投资人，包括一些非常好的，对投资 Faire 感到紧张。

speaker: Unknown
en: If they frame the question slightly differently, which is, "Is Max capable of being a world-class entrepreneur?"
zh: 如果他们稍微不同地构建这个问题，也就是，“Max 有能力成为世界级的企业家吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: the answer was yes.
zh: 答案是肯定的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So again, it's a tactic you have to understand, what exactly am I trying to extract?
zh: 所以再说一次，这是一种你必须理解的策略，我到底在试图提取什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Same person, wrong question, wrong result.
zh: 同一个人，错误的问题，错误的结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: And many people passed on Fire and they regret it.
zh: 很多人错过了 Fire，他们很后悔。

speaker: Unknown
en: And these are actually quite talented investors.
zh: 这些实际上是非常有天赋的投资人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They just didn't frame the question correctly when they were calling someone like Jack Dorsey up for the reference.
zh: 他们只是在给 Jack Dorsey 这样的人打电话做背景参考时，没有正确地构建问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Any other questions you find really helpful in extracting the right information?
zh: 还有其他你觉得在提取正确信息时真的很有帮助的问题吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: When I interview candidates for senior people in leadership positions, I always ask them, look at whatever company they're at and say, "If you were CEO, what would you have done differently?"
zh: 当我面试领导层高级职位的候选人时，我总是问他们，看看他们所在的任何公司并说，“如果你是 CEO，你会做哪些不同的做法？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you get a feel for their strategic mindset of can they drive value creation?
zh: 你会对他们的战略思维有感觉，看他们能否推动价值创造？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because almost by definition, they've come from a company that's had some traction success, so can they edit?
zh: 因为几乎根据定义，他们来自一家有一定吸引力的公司，所以他们能做编辑吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So in your case, I would've asked you, "If you were CEO of Airbnb, what would you have done differently?"
zh: 所以在你的案例中，我会问你，“如果你是 Airbnb 的 CEO，你会做哪些不同的做法？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you learn a lot from that question.
zh: 你会从这个问题中学到很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: On references specifically, I think a general arc that's pretty good is asking the person, what would lead to this person being most successful?
zh: 具体在背景调查上，我认为一个通常很好的切入点是问这个人，什么会导致这个人取得最大成功？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if something were not to work out, what would be the primary root cause that you can identify if something going wrong?
zh: 如果如果有事情不顺利，你能识别出的主要根本原因是什么，如果出问题了的话？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think generally probing on those two arcs leads to a lot of insight.
zh: 我认为通常探测这两个切入点会产生很多洞察。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that first question is for the candidate or is that in the reference?
zh: 而第一个问题是给候选人的还是那是背景调查里的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, for the candidate.
zh: 是的，给候选人。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's for the candidate specifically.
zh: 那是专门给候选人的。

speaker: Lenny
en: Got it.
zh: 明白了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I don't want them to criticize Airbnb.
zh: 因为我不希望他们批评 Airbnb。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think that's that productive, but you can tell how much of the current business model have they absorbed, how much do they understand trade-offs.
zh: 我不认为那有多大的成效，但你可以看出他们吸收了多少当前的商业模式，他们理解多少权衡。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then can they create an unfair advantage?
zh: 然后他们能创造不公平优势吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because they have insights into afterburners.
zh: 因为他们对加速器有洞察。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I have a follow-up question, which is usually gold, which is, let's say I asked you this question about Airbnb and you give me this great answer.
zh: 然后我有一个追问问题，通常很有用，也就是，假设我问了你这个关于 Airbnb 的问题而你给了我这个很好的回答。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm like, "Well, why weren't you able to persuade Brian to do it?"
zh: 我会说，“嗯，为什么你没能说服 Brian 那样做？”

speaker: Unknown
en: So you made this interesting point that there's tactics that can help you get better at finding and identifying talent.
zh: 所以你提出了这个有趣的观点，有一些策略可以帮助你更好地发现和识别人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of this is just the feedback loop of doing it a bunch, it sounds like.
zh: 其中很多只是大量做这件事的反馈循环，听起来是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: I find the feedback loop is so, it's hard to actually ...
zh: 我发现反馈循环是如此，实际上很难……

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people interview, hire, and then don't really learn much from how it ends up going.
zh: 大多数人面试、雇佣，然后并没有从结果如何中学到多少东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this gut thing that happens, but they're not really thinking about it.
zh: 发生了一种直觉的东西，但他们并没有真正思考它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have any advice for just how to make the most out of the lesson of seeing how something went?
zh: 对于如何充分利用观察事情进展的教训，你有什么建议吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: So I've read some research on the topic.
zh: 所以我读过一些关于这个主题的研究。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you ask yourself 30 days after any hire, "Would you make the same decision?"
zh: 如果你在任何一次招聘的 30 天后问自己，“你会做出同样的决定吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: That 30-day loop is pretty useful and it basically it's as accurate as measuring in a year or two years out.
zh: 那个 30 天的循环非常有用，基本上它和在一两年后测量一样准确。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you get a pretty tight feedback loop and you can ask the entire hiring team.
zh: 所以你得到了一个非常紧凑的反馈循环，你可以问整个招聘团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that is just a technique that every company should use.
zh: 所以我认为这只是每家公司都应该使用的一种技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to talk about this framework that you have, barrels and ammunition, because this is really mind-expanding and helping people understand who to even hire.
zh: 我想谈谈你有的这个框架，桶和弹药，因为这真的是开拓思维并帮助人们理解到底该雇佣谁。

speaker: Unknown
en: So look, most companies raise money, they have some traction, they do a seed round, they get launched, they get some traction.
zh: 所以看，大多数公司筹集资金，他们有一些吸引力，他们做了一轮种子融资，他们发布了，他们获得了一些吸引力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then they raise a lot of money, whether it's a series A or a Series B.
zh: 然后他们筹集很多钱，无论是 A 轮还是 B 轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they hire a lot of people infallibly or at least historically.
zh: 然后他们不可避免地雇佣很多人，或者至少在历史上是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the CEO, almost without accession, gets frustrated because they've hired a lot of people.
zh: 然后 CEO，几乎无一例外地，感到沮丧，因为他们雇佣了很多人。

speaker: Unknown
en: The burn rate has increased a lot and they don't feel like more is getting accomplished per unit of time, per day, per week, per month, per quarter, and they get frustrated.
zh: 烧钱率增加了很多，而他们觉得在单位时间内，每天、每周、每月、每季度，并没有完成更多的事情，他们感到很沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so then they sit around at a dinner with other CEOs or people like me or one-on-one conversation with me and are incredibly unhappy and disappointed that I'm spending all this money on all these people, but we're getting less done or the same done.
zh: 于是他们和其他 CEO 或像我这样的人坐在晚宴上，或者和我一对一交谈时，对我花这么多钱在这些员工身上感到极度不满和失望，但我们完成的工作变少了，或者完成的和以前一样多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And why, why, why, why?
zh: 为什么，为什么，为什么，为什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: After years of sitting through these conversations at dinner with other CEOs or COOs, I realized that the fundamental driver of this is that the number of people that can independently drive an initiative from beginning, from inception to success is very limited within any company.
zh: 在和其他 CEO 或 COO 的晚宴上经历过这些对话多年后，我意识到其中的根本驱动力是，能够独立推动一个计划从开始，从构想到成功完成的人数在任何公司内部都是非常有限的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you hire more people without expanding the number of what I call burials that can drive from inception to success, all you're doing is stacking people behind the same initiatives.
zh: 如果你雇佣了更多的人，而没有增加我所谓的能从构想到成功推动的「burials」，你实际上只是在同一个计划背后堆积人员。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you're wasting time, energy, and increasing your collaboration tax, your coordination tax.
zh: 所以你在浪费时间、精力，并增加了你的协作税、协调税。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's what causes the drag coefficient.
zh: 这就是造成阻力系数的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, at PayPal, we had about 254 people in Mountain View when we were acquired.
zh: 举个例子，在 PayPal，当我们被收购时，我们在 Mountain View 大约有 254 人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Of those people, depending on how strict you really want to be.
zh: 在这些人中，取决于你想多么严格地计算。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is considered one of the best talent rich networks of all time in technology, there's between 12 to 17 barrels in the organization.
zh: 这被认为是科技史上最丰富的人才网络之一，组织中有 12 到 17 个 barrels。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's an infinite number.
zh: 这是一个巨大的数字。

speaker: Unknown
en: I once asked Jack Altman on a podcast at Loudouns, which is a pretty damn big company, how many barrels of the company?
zh: 我有一次在 Loudouns 的播客上问 Jack Altman，那是一家相当大的公司，公司有多少 barrels？

speaker: Unknown
en: The answer was two.
zh: 答案是两个。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a more common answer for a very good company.
zh: 对于一家非常好的公司来说，这是一个更普遍的答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you have between two and let's say 15 barrels at a company.
zh: 所以在一个公司里，你有两个到比如说 15 个 barrels。

speaker: Unknown
en: That defines the unique number of things you can do in parallel versus sequentially.
zh: 这定义了你可以并行与串行执行的独特事务数量。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just hiring more people is not going to change that.
zh: 仅仅雇佣更多的人并不会改变这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if anything, it's just going to cause a collaboration, a coordination tax, and you're going to have a track revision, you're going to do less.
zh: 如果有什么影响的话，这只会造成协作、协调税，并且你会有一个修正，你会做得更少。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the key is to me is if you want to do more or need to do more, your market requires you to do more, your business model requires you to do more, VCs require you to do more, you need to have more barrels.
zh: 所以对我来说关键是，如果你想做得更多或者需要做得更多，你的市场要求你做得更多，你的商业模式要求你做得更多，VC 要求你做得更多，你需要拥有更多的 barrels。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, the question is how and when and all this.
zh: 现在，问题是如何以及何时，以及所有这一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of details there, but fundamentally, the ratio of barrels to ammunition is what dictates the number of important initiatives that can be pursued simultaneously.
zh: 有很多细节，但根本上，barrels 与 ammunition 的比例决定了可以同时进行的重要计划的数量。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're not saying you don't want ammunition.
zh: 你并不是说你不需要 ammunition。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's valuable.
zh: 它很有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: To make an impact, you need ammunition in addition to the barrel.
zh: 为了产生影响力，除了 barrel 之外，你还需要 ammunition。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, you definitely need ammunition.
zh: 是的，你肯定需要 ammunition。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it depends on what kind of project.
zh: 这取决于什么样的项目。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are types of projects where an individual barrel may be able to succeed with very limited or no ammunition.
zh: 有些类型的项目，一个单独的 barrel 可能能够在很少或没有 ammunition 的情况下成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes you may need a designer, an engineering team, a PM, a data analysis, blah, blah, blah.
zh: 有时你可能需要一名设计师、一个工程团队、一名 PM、数据分析，等等，等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: Depends on what the project is, what the problem you're trying to solve is, what's the proper amount of ammunition?
zh: 取决于项目是什么，你想解决的问题是什么，什么是适当的 ammunition 数量？

speaker: Unknown
en: But once you think about the ratios of ammunition to the problem, you can be much more constructive and deliberate and intentional about the team construction.
zh: 但一旦你考虑了 ammunition 与问题的比例，你就可以对团队构建更加建设性、深思熟虑和有意识。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people hearing this assume they are barrels.
zh: 大多数听到这个的人都假设他们是 barrels。

speaker: Unknown
en: What helps you understand if someone's truly a barrel?
zh: 什么能帮助你理解某人是否真的是 barrel？

speaker: Unknown
en: Can they take an idea and make it happen?
zh: 他们能拿出一个想法并实现它吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Basically, we're going up that, there's a hill over there, that's the hill.
zh: 基本上，我们就是在往上走，那边有一座山，那就是那座山。

speaker: Unknown
en: Get us over that hill.
zh: 带我们越过那座山。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one way or the other, they will motivate people if they need to, they will accumulate resources if they need to, they will measure what they need to, and they're going to get your company across that hill.
zh: 不管怎样，如果他们需要，他们会激励人们，如果他们需要，他们会积累资源，如果他们需要，他们会衡量事物，他们会把你的公司带过那座山。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a barrel.
zh: 那就是 barrel。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything less than that is not a barrel.
zh: 除此之外的都不是 barrel。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this is skills like internal org stuff, resource.
zh: 所以这涉及到像内部组织事务、资源这样的技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the collection of all the things to get something done.
zh: 这是完成一件事所需的所有东西的集合。

speaker: Unknown
en: A collection of all those things, so it's basically there's an outcome.
zh: 所有这些东西的集合，所以基本上这就是有一个结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: CEO, founder wants an outcome and come hell or high water, this person is going to deliver that outcome.
zh: CEO、创始人想要一个结果，不管困难多大，这个人都将交付那个结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then the outcome can be fairly narrow and not that difficult in the beginning.
zh: 然后这个结果一开始可能相当狭隘且不那么困难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you expand the scope, the complexity, the difficulty that you basically entrust to your barrels.
zh: 然后你扩大你基本上委托给你的 barrels 的范围、复杂性、难度。

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes they have no line of sight of how to solve it when you start.
zh: 有时他们在开始时没有如何解决它的视线。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes you have a preliminary idea.
zh: 有时你有一个初步的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it ranges, but ultimately it's that skill of, I'm going to take this off your plate, you can fire and forget, and this is going to happen.
zh: 所以情况各异，但归根结底这是一种技能，就是我会把这个从你的盘子上拿走，你可以发射后不管，这就会发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if it's not going to happen, I'm going to come back to you proactively with the issues I'm confronting, what I've already tried, the diagnosis of the root causes and ask for your help with sufficient time for you to intervene and try to brainstorm with me to get us to the right answer.
zh: 如果这不会发生，我会主动回到你这里，带上我面临的问题，我已经尝试过的做法，根本原因的诊断，并在你有足够的时间干预和尝试与我头脑风暴以帮助我们找到正确答案之前寻求你的帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Agency is the word that comes to mind when you talk about this role.
zh: 智能体是谈论这个角色时会想到的词。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think agency is accurate.
zh: 是的，我认为智能体是准确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The problem I have with terms like agency is it's a little bit like strategy.
zh: 我对像智能体这样的术语的问题在于，它有点像战略。

speaker: Unknown
en: It goes in one ear, a lot of people and out the other ear and they don't really process the meaning.
zh: 它进一只耳朵，很多人然后从另一只耳朵出去，他们并没有真正理解其中的含义。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Who are some examples of barrels that make this real so people can understand what you're talking about?
zh: 有哪些“木桶”的例子能让它变得真实，以便人们能理解你在谈论什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I talked to my YC lecturer in 2014 about how to operate.
zh: 2014年，我和我的 YC 讲师谈论过如何运营。

speaker: Unknown
en: They can be as simple as the now somewhat famous in technology smoothie test, which is, we used to have engineers work pretty hard at Square and pretty late.
zh: 它们可以像现在科技界 somewhat 著名的思慕雪测试一样简单，也就是，我们过去常常让工程师在 Square 努力工作到很晚。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I always wanted them to have food so they wouldn't be famished, they wouldn't be distracted.
zh: 我一直希望他们能有食物，这样他们就不会挨饿，也不会分心。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I didn't really want them to eat junk food because I actually think junk food's bad for you, bad for your brain, et cetera.
zh: 我并不真的想让他们吃垃圾食品，因为我实际上认为垃圾食品对你有害，对大脑有害，等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: So settled on delivery and really wanted to provide a 9:00 PM cold smoothies.
zh: 所以决定叫外卖，真的很想在晚上9点提供冰冷的思慕雪。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we had, at that time, a pretty substantial team at Square, office team, EAs, this was not a lean, mean organization.
zh: 而且我们那时在 Square 有一个相当庞大的团队，办公团队、行政助理，这并不是一个精简、高效的 organization。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I tried through the office team EAs and nothing ... We never got healthy, delicious, and cold smoothies delivered at 9:00 PM.
zh: 所以我尝试通过办公团队和行政助理去做，结果什么也没……我们从未在晚上9点成功送达健康、美味且冰冷的思慕雪。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just kept that one.
zh: 就只保留了那一个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was getting frustrating because if the smoothies aren't cold, then no one's going to eat them.
zh: 我变得很沮丧，因为如果思慕雪不冷，就没人会去吃。

speaker: Unknown
en: If they don't arrive at 9:00, then no one can really bank on their refreshments.
zh: 如果它们不在9点到达，就没人能真正指望这些茶点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything went wrong.
zh: 一切都出了岔子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I had this intern named Taylor Francis, and I was explaining just my frustration.
zh: 然后我有一个名叫 Taylor Francis 的实习生，我正在解释我的挫败感。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was his second day at work.
zh: 那是他工作的第二天。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's like, "I'll solve it."
zh: 他说，“我来解决这个问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, "Okay, kid, good luck with that."
zh: 我说，“好吧，孩子，祝你好运。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I was like, "Sure, keep trying."
zh: 我说，“当然，继续尝试。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Anyway, day goes by, 9 o'clock arrives.
zh: 无论如何，一天过去了，9点钟到了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And lo and behold, smoothies show up at 9:00 PM, delivered on the standing desk table where the engineers would congregate.
zh: 瞧，思慕雪在晚上9点送到了，放在工程师们聚集的站立式办公桌上。

speaker: Unknown
en: I sample them, they're cold, they taste great.
zh: 我尝了尝，它们是冷的，味道很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm like, "Oh my God, I found a barrel."
zh: 我说，“天哪，我发现了一个木桶。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I later gave him almost everything to do.
zh: 后来我几乎把所有事情都交给他去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to go back to actually the first question.
zh: 我想回到实际上第一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: You shared some amazing advice for how to identify great talent, but I'm still curious, when you find that barrel, for example, when everyone's throwing money at them, there's all these amazing teams to join.
zh: 你分享了一些关于如何识别伟大人才的惊人建议，但我仍然很好奇，当你发现那个木桶时，例如，当每个人都向他们撒钱时，有这么多很棒的团队可以加入。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are some things that companies do to attract and convince them to join their team?
zh: 公司会做些什么来吸引并说服他们加入他们的团队？

speaker: Unknown
en: The standard of stuff is still true.
zh: 标准的东西仍然是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Mission, selling the vision to mission is indispensable.
zh: 使命，把愿景 sell 给使命是不可或缺的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most people who have proven talent anyway, at least in the current world, are going to attract offers from multiple opportunities.
zh: 大多数拥有经证实才能的人，至少在当前世界，都会收到多个机会的录用通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so you've got to convince them that your opportunity is very special.
zh: 所以你必须让他们相信你的机会是非常特别的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one way to do that that's a little bit more nuanced is convince them that their particular skill overlaps with the critical blockers to the current company, meaning they're betting on themselves.
zh: 我认为做到这一点的一种稍微更微妙的方法是，让他们相信他们的特殊技能与当前公司的关键阻碍重叠，这意味着他们在押注自己。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, if they are superb at, let's say marketing, if the biggest blocker of the company in the company's current success is not technology, not the product, but we believe it's marketing, it's really easy to go to a world-class marketing person and say, "Not only is this great company building something really cool and interesting that you'll be proud of, but your particular ability is very unique and differentiated and you can solve this."
zh: 所以举个例子，如果他们非常擅长，比如说市场营销，如果公司当前成功的最大阻碍不是技术，不是产品，而是我们认为它是市场营销，那么去拜访一位世界级的营销人员就很容易说，“这不仅是一家构建非常酷且有趣东西的伟大公司，你会为此感到自豪，而且你的特殊能力是非常独特和差异化的，你可以解决这个问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: This is actually how I wound up at Square.
zh: 这实际上就是我最终来到 Square 的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: Back in 2010, I was actually, I'd just been acquihired into Google and was planning on being a VC actually next after I was investing whenever Google is going to compensate us.
zh: 回到2010年，我实际上，我刚被收购雇佣进入 Google，并计划实际上接下来做一名 VC，在我投资 Google 什么时候会补偿我们之后。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the investors in Square called me up and they said, "Hey, we've been looking for almost a year now for someone who knows something about financial services yet is still an entrepreneurial."
zh: 然后 Square 的投资者给我打电话，他们说，“嘿，我们差不多寻找了一年，寻找一个懂金融服务但仍然具有企业家精神的人。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're like, "Hey, there's only three of these."
zh: 他们说，“嘿，这样的人只有三个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: At the time, 2010, it was like, "There's only really two or three of you in the world, so would you be interested?"
zh: 那时，2010年，情况是，“世界上真的只有两三个你这样的人，所以你感兴趣吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I said, "Well, maybe."
zh: 我说，“嗯，也许吧。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But that was the argument to me that made me leave Google early after two weeks and infuriate everybody.
zh: 但这就是让我在两周后提前离开 Google 并激怒所有人的论点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And bypass Venture for another three years, which had been my plan, was because they made the argument that, hey, I was one of three people in the world that could actually do this job.
zh: 并让我放弃 Venture 另外三年，那曾是我的计划，是因为他们提出了这个论点，嘿，我是世界上仅有的三个能真正做这份工作的人之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: So look, there's a, I don't know if it's ego, but it's also just impact.
zh: 所以看，这有一个，我不知道这是否是自我，但也只是影响力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yep.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, exactly.
zh: 嗯，确切地说。

speaker: Unknown
en: Impact, you have talent, you want to use them and you want to feel that you're challenged every day and that what you're doing really, really matters.
zh: 影响力，你有才华，你想使用它们，你想感到你每天都在受到挑战，而且你正在做的事情真的非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that can be extremely helpful.
zh: 所以我认为这可以非常有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: My more important arc in this is I think you have to build a company on undiscovered talent.
zh: 我在这方面更重要的观点是，我认为你必须建立在未被发现的才华之上。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think you really want to compete for the people that everybody else wants.
zh: 我并不认为你真的想要去竞争那些其他所有人都想要的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I learned this at PayPal.
zh: 我在 PayPal 学到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter taught me this literally the first week of my job at PayPal, that the way to build a company, we were jogging around the Stanford campuses, you've got to find these undiscovered talent.
zh: Peter 在我在 PayPal 工作的第一周就字面意义上教会了我这一点，关于如何建立一家公司，我们在斯坦福校园周围慢跑，你必须找到这些未被发现的才华。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the only way to scale an organization against these large encumbers with infinite money, et cetera.
zh: 这是将一个组织与这些拥有无限资金的大型负担进行规模抗衡的唯一途径,等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I've been on that crusade for 25 years.
zh: 我为此进行了 25 年的奋战。

speaker: Unknown
en: For those who are interested, you can link to it.
zh: 对那些感兴趣的人,你可以链接到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: I gave a speech at Ramp, how to hire, the talks in detail.
zh: 我在 Ramp 发表过一次演讲,关于如何招聘,详细地谈了这些。

speaker: Unknown
en: I also recommend Eric, CEO of Ramp's speech, which is fairly similar.
zh: 我也推荐 Eric,Ramp 的 CEO 的演讲,那是相当相似的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Both videos are online.
zh: 两个视频都在网上。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's such interesting advice and it makes so much sense.
zh: 这是如此有趣的建议,而且非常有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're not going to be able to afford the people that have done the thing at top companies.
zh: 你将无法雇佣得起那些在大公司做过这件事的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And also they're just probably not the people to join at early start, right?
zh: 而且他们可能也不是那种会加入早期初创公司的人,对吧?

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, they're also not maybe the people you want, so there's adverse election, but it's like a salary cap.
zh: 嗯,他们可能也不是你想要的人,所以存在逆向选择,但这就像是一个工资帽。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most sports these days have salary caps.
zh: 如今大多数体育运动都有工资帽。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you're a startup, not only do you have a salary cap, you probably have one 10th the salary cap of the people you're competing with.
zh: 当你是一家初创公司时,你不仅有工资帽,你的工资帽可能只有你竞争对手的十分之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you've got to figure out how to leverage less assets to more success.
zh: 所以你必须弄清楚如何利用更少的资产来获得更大的成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's just one tip when you're looking for undiscovered talent that's a sign of, okay, this person is really special?
zh: 在寻找未被发掘的人才时,有什么线索表明,好吧,这个人真的很特别?

speaker: Unknown
en: I know you have a lot to talk about here, but just what's one tip?
zh: 我知道你在这方面有很多要谈的,但只要一条建议?

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's basically isolating why other people aren't going to process them correctly.
zh: 我认为这基本上是隔离为什么其他人无法正确处理他们的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: Most recruiting at large organizations becomes a homogenous function.
zh: 大多数大型组织的招聘变成了一个同质化的功能。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if you understand why this person is going to get thrown into this black box thing and not get processed accurately, it's pretty easy.
zh: 所以如果你理解为什么这个人会被扔进这个黑盒子里而不能被准确处理,这就很简单了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I always think about, let's say this person was interviewing at Meta or Google or Block these days or Coinbase, what are they going to miss and then why?
zh: 所以我总是思考,假设这个人在 Meta 或 Google 或 Block 或 Coinbase 面试,他们会错过什么,然后为什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: And then that leads to, oh, perfect.
zh: 然后这就导致,噢,完美。

speaker: Unknown
en: So sometimes it's just lack of information.
zh: 所以有时这只是缺乏信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it's controversial to say this, but one of the reasons why the net impact of my higher undiscovered talent is you wind up skewing younger.
zh: 有时说这个有争议,但我雇佣未被发掘的人才的净影响的原因之一是你最终会更偏向年轻人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not because you need young people, it's that younger people have, by definition, less data.
zh: 这不是因为你需要年轻人,而是因为年轻人,根据定义,拥有更少的数据。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like we use credit scoring, FICA scores.
zh: 就像我们使用信用评分,FICA 分数。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the same thing for employment.
zh: 对于就业来说也是一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: By the time you're over 30-some odd things, there's so many data points about you that this black box machine is usually going to process you like many other people.
zh: 当你超过 30 多岁时,关于你有很多数据点,这个黑盒机器通常会像处理其他人一样处理你。

speaker: Unknown
en: If there's no data points, it's very hard for a black box machine that does homogenous evaluation to evaluate you.
zh: 如果没有数据点,对于做同质化评估的黑盒机器来说,很难评估你。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there is alpha, so to speak, by definition for people who have no data points.
zh: 所以就所谓的阿尔法而言,对于没有数据点的人来说,根据定义是存在的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting how this is the same skill as being an investor, picking startups to invest in.
zh: 有趣的是这与作为投资者的技能是一样的,挑选初创公司进行投资。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, absolutely.
zh: 是的,绝对是。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to talk about something else.
zh: 我想谈谈别的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I asked a few people that know you well that work with you at various companies what to talk to you about.
zh: 我问了一些很了解你、在不同公司与你共事过的人要和你谈什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one person said that when I asked him what to talk about, he said, "My immediate reaction is that he is a bar raiser.
zh: 有一个人说当我问他要谈什么时,他说,“我的直接反应是他是一个提升标准的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: No matter what kind of numbers we put up, he pushes us to do more.
zh: 无论我们交出什么样的数字,他都推动我们做得更多。

speaker: Unknown
en: In fact, often it seems like the better we do, the harder he pushes."
zh: 事实上,通常似乎我们做得越好,他推得越狠。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Does that resonate?
zh: 这有共鸣吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think that's true.
zh: 是的,我认为这是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I think, look, ultimately, I'll channel someone else's feedback, but it's in the same vein.
zh: 我的意思是,我认为,看,归根结底,我会引用别人的反馈,但也是一样的道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: So a friend of mine who's a CEO once asked Mike Moore, it's like, "What's the most common denominator of the best CEOs ever?"
zh: 所以我的一位担任 CEO 的朋友曾经问 Mike Moore,就像是,“有史以来最好的 CEO 的最大共同点是什么?”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he said, "It's the relentless application of force."
zh: 他说,“是力量的无情运用。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Quote.
zh: 引用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's the job of the CEO.
zh: 我认为这是 CEO 的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: People eventually get comfortable, complacent.
zh: 人们最终会变得舒适、自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: The more success you have, the more complacent the organization tends to get, and the single role for the CEO is offsetting that complacency.
zh: 你越成功,组织往往变得越自满,而 CEO 的唯一角色就是抵消这种自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: So to the point, the more success you have, the better you're doing, the more complacency naturally kicks in.
zh: 所以关键在于,你越成功,你做得越好,自然就会产生越多的自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: And unless you've erected a network effect, you do not want to get complacent.
zh: 除非你已经建立了网络效应,否则你不想变得自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even then, debate whether you should.
zh: 即使如此,也要辩论你是否应该自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: But fundamentally, most businesses are not network-effect businesses.
zh: 但从根本上说,大多数企业不是网络效应企业。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're not going to run on their own for a long time.
zh: 它们不会在很长一段时间内自行运转。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that's one insight is the better you're doing, the more the CEO should push.
zh: 所以我认为这是一个洞察，你做得越好，CEO 就应该越多地施压。

speaker: Unknown
en: Secondly, it's a little bit like sports when you're growing up.
zh: 其次，这有点像你成长过程中的体育运动。

speaker: Unknown
en: People, when they're winning, take advantage of feedback better than when they're losing usually.
zh: 人们通常在赢的时候比输的时候更能利用反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, now what I do is mostly a VC, mostly a board member, mostly a consigliere to a founder.
zh: 所以举例来说，我现在做的主要是 VC，主要是董事会成员，主要是创始人的 consigliere。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe what's less intuitive, and you may picked up on this in your research and interviews of people who know me, when a company's struggling, I'm actually usually very non-critical and more like a coach and supporter.
zh: 可能有点不那么直观，你在对认识我的人的研究和采访中可能已经注意到了，当一家公司陷入困境时，我实际上通常非常不挑剔，更像是一个教练和支持者。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because the company, the founder knows they're struggling.
zh: 因为公司，创始人知道他们在挣扎。

speaker: Unknown
en: Being critical doesn't really help them solve the problems.
zh: 批评真的不能帮他们解决问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's when being supportive can actually somewhat counterintuitively be more important.
zh: 在这种情况下，给予支持实际上有点反直觉地变得更加重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: But when the company's thriving, it's really important to be critical and isolating things that will eventually be problems while everybody in the company's really happy and borderline complacent.
zh: 但当公司蓬勃发展时，挑剔和孤立那些最终会成为问题的东西真的很重要，即使公司里的每个人都非常快乐，甚至有点自满。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you want to be the opposite as a default.
zh: 所以作为默认，你想要相反的做法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's like a really good sports coach.
zh: 这就像一个非常好的体育教练。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you're winning is when to polish everything and really master the details.
zh: 当你赢的时候，才是打磨一切并真正掌握细节的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you're losing, you definitely also have to be exciting people and embracing the future and selling the future.
zh: 当你输的时候，你也肯定必须激励人们，拥抱未来并推销未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: So is, say someone's listening, a founder or a product leader, the advice here is just keep pushing harder, set the bar higher as things, even if you're doing great?
zh: 所以如果有听众，创始人或产品负责人，这里的建议就是继续更努力地推动，把门槛设得更高，即使你做得很好？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, if you're doing great.
zh: 是的，如果你做得很好的话。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, also you have to remember, I remember giving a speech once at Square.
zh: 另外，你必须记住，我记得有一次在 Square 发表过演讲。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like you get to a certain threshold, crazy inflection.
zh: 就像你达到了某个阈值，疯狂的拐点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Momentum gets you to certain valuation and all these attributes.
zh: 势头会给你带来一定的估值和所有这些属性。

speaker: Unknown
en: But it's like winning a Super Bowl.
zh: 但这就像赢得超级碗。

speaker: Unknown
en: The last year was great.
zh: 去年很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: Last four quarters was wonderful.
zh: 过去的四个季度很精彩。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like winning the Superbowl.
zh: 就像赢得超级碗一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: You got to come back next year and start your records zero, zero again.
zh: 你明年必须回来，你的记录从零开始，再次从零开始。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you got to remember that.
zh: 你必须记住这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Actually, Ventures like that.
zh: 实际上，Ventures 也是这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm only as good as my last investment.
zh: 我只跟我最近的投资一样好。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've had 13 years or whatever, of pretty damn good investments.
zh: 我有 13 年或者 whatever，相当不错的投资记录。

speaker: Unknown
en: But truthfully, I have to wake up every day and find some undiscovered founder that's going to change the world.
zh: 但老实说，我每天都必须醒来，找到某个未被发现的创始人，去改变世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if I don't do that, it doesn't matter what I've done in the last 13 years.
zh: 如果我不那样做，我在过去 13 年里做了什么并不重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a company's like that and the company can skate on autopilot for a while.
zh: 公司也是这样，公司可以在 autopilot 上滑行一段时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: Venture, you really can't ever skate.
zh: 在 Ventures 领域，你真的永远不能 skate。

speaker: Unknown
en: I definitely saw this with Brian Chesky.
zh: 我在 Brian Chesky 身上肯定看到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: He just felt like things were going great.
zh: 他只是觉得事情进展得很顺利。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we just shipped amazing products and growth is up and he's just always pedal to the metal no matter what.
zh: 我们刚刚发布了令人惊叹的产品，增长了，他就只是无论什么都把油门踩到底。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like, "Come on, when are we going to take a little break?"
zh: 就像，“来吧，我们什么时候稍微休息一下？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's interesting because when we did have little breaks here and there, morales actually went down because people are like, "What am I working on?
zh: 这很有趣，因为当我们这里那里确实有小休息时，morale 实际上下降了，因为人们就像，“我在做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not that exciting."
zh: 这不是很令人兴奋。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Brian and I are usually in sync a lot.
zh: Brian 和我通常非常同步。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a really good interview when I interviewed him also at the same conference of how to hire, when he talks mostly about founder mode.
zh: 有一个非常好的采访，当时我在同一个会议上采访他关于如何招聘，他主要谈论创始人模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I generally subscribe to virtually all of Brian's views.
zh: 但我几乎认同 Brian 的所有观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: He even taught me some of these things himself.
zh: 他甚至亲自教了我其中一些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the more important point I think you identified, which is very subtle, is really talented people are like superb athletes.
zh: 但我认为你指出的更重要的一点，这非常微妙，那就是真正有才华的人就像超级运动员一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when things are going well and people are really coasting, they're not happy.
zh: 当事情进展顺利，人们真正在 coasting 时，他们并不快乐。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have an internal clock tempo.
zh: 他们有一个内部的时钟节奏。

speaker: Unknown
en: They just want to create things and create value and drive, drive.
zh: 他们只是想创造东西，创造价值，drive，drive。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the morale actually does go down for the best people in the world when people are skating.
zh: 当人们在 skating 时，世界上最好的人的士气确实会下降。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So actually along those lines, there's a lot of anxiety in the market, in the job market about the future of careers.
zh: 所以实际上顺着这些思路，市场上，就业市场上有很多关于职业未来的焦虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: Am I going to have a job?
zh: 我会有工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where are things going?
zh: 事情将去向何方？

speaker: Unknown
en: And it just feels like people are working very, very hard.
zh: 感觉大家都在非常、非常努力地工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're just putting in a lot of hours, especially the most AI people.
zh: 他们投入了大量时间，尤其是那些最懂 AI 的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: It just feels like they're working harder than ever.
zh: 感觉他们比以往任何时候都更努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if you saw this thing Tyler Cohen put out of just work harder.
zh: 不知道你有没有看到 Tyler Cohen 发布的那个东西，就是关于更努力工作的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now is the time to work harder because AI is eating away at your value.
zh: 现在是时候更努力工作了，因为 AI 正在蚕食你的价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: You probably talked to a lot of people looking for career advice of just like, "This feels scary and I feel like I'm working too hard and what should I do?"
zh: 你可能和很多寻求职业建议的人谈过，比如“这感觉好可怕，我觉得自己工作太辛苦了，我该怎么办？”

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know.
zh: 我不知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have any just advice for folks from-
zh: 你有什么建议给那些——

speaker: Keith Rabois
en: Well, I do think AI is going to radically reorient lots of people's careers, maybe including mine. So I think that's actually true. And I think the way to thrive in a rapidly emerging technology world is to be intellectually curious. So for example, I'm a business person historically. I did actually code when I was really young, but basically professionally, just a business person. What I've noticed in some of the best organizations is the number one consumer of tokens is the CMO-
zh: 好吧，我确实认为 AI 将从根本上改变许多人的职业道路，可能也包括我的。所以我认为这实际上是真的。我认为在一个快速新兴的技术世界中蓬勃发展的方法是保持智力上的好奇心。举个例子，我历史上是个商人。我小时候确实写过代码，但基本上在职业层面，只是个商人。我注意到在一些最好的组织中，token 的第一大消费者是 CMO——

speaker: Unknown
en: CMO.
zh: CMO。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... because these people are intellectually curious.
zh: ……因为这些人在智力上充满好奇。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so they're like, "Wow, there's all these cool things I can do now with my hands.
zh: 所以他们会想，“哇，我现在可以用双手做所有这些很酷的事情了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Either I had to rely on other teams or never got access the way I wanted and blah, blah, blah or lag."
zh: 我以前必须依赖其他团队，或者无法以我想要的方式获得访问权限，诸如此类的废话或者滞后。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And they just do it.
zh: 然后他们就动手去做了。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is actually true at Opendoor.
zh: 这在 Opendoor 实际上是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's true at another great company that I'm on the board of that's incredible.
zh: 在我所任职的另一家很棒的董事会公司里也是真的，那家公司非常不可思议。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think you can be intellectually curious and future-proof yourself more than just, yes, you can work harder and a big subscriber to no days off and working all the time and all that stuff.
zh: 所以我认为你可以保持智力上的好奇心并让自己面向未来，而不仅仅是，是的，你可以更努力工作，并且是“没有休息日”和一直工作这一套的忠实信徒。

speaker: Unknown
en: But fundamentally, the intellectual curiosity is, "I want to learn new things," and that is how you embrace the future.
zh: 但从根本上说，智力好奇心是“我想学习新事物”，这就是你拥抱未来的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you said CMO is who's the most-
zh: 你刚才说 CMO 是谁最——

speaker: Keith Rabois
en: CMO. In both of these two companies-
zh: CMO。在这两家公司里——

speaker: Unknown
en: So interesting.
zh: 真有意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... both massive, awesome companies with lots of engineers.
zh: ……两家都是拥有很多工程师的大型、很棒的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's very encouraging.
zh: 我认为这非常令人鼓舞。

speaker: Unknown
en: For the executive particularly, like, "This is definitely the best executive in the company."
zh: 对于高管来说，尤其是，“这绝对是公司里最好的高管。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And what are they building?
zh: 他们在构建什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it landing pages and paid tests?
zh: 是落地页和付费测试吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, sometimes it's more like what we would've thought of analytics.
zh: 嗯，有时更像我们所想的分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it's actually campaigns, actual campaigns.
zh: 有时实际上是营销活动，实际的营销活动。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just like they don't need to rely upon deputies and deputies and deputies to get actual work product.
zh: 就是他们不需要依靠一层又一层的代理人来完成实际的工作成果。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so they're just shipping things or shipping drafts of things or giving the CEO insights into things themselves.
zh: 所以他们只是在发布东西，或者发布东西的草稿，或者自己向 CEO 提供对事物的见解。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to get your take on the future of specifically the product triad.
zh: 我想听听你对具体产品铁三角未来的看法。

speaker: Unknown
en: You work with a lot of product people, engineers, designers.
zh: 你与很多产品人员、工程师、设计师一起工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone's always wondering, what the hell is going to happen in my career?
zh: 大家总是想知道，我的职业生涯到底会发生什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Thoughts on just the future of those three specific roles?
zh: 对这三个具体角色的未来有什么想法？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I saw this podcast or I listened to this podcast that Peter Fenn did.
zh: 好吧，我看了这个播客或者听了 Peter Fenn 做的这个播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he convinced me that the idea of a PM makes no sense basically in the future.
zh: 他让我相信，PM（产品经理）这个概念在未来基本上讲不通。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about decomposing the logic is what does PM usually do?
zh: 如果你思考一下解构逻辑，就是 PM 通常做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: They take these inputs from customers, they create this sequential roadmap that's well organized over the next year, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.
zh: 他们从客户那里获取这些输入，他们创建一个按顺序排列的路线图，在下一年组织得很好，诸如此类的废话。

speaker: Unknown
en: That world is ridiculous.
zh: 那个世界是很荒谬的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right now, the capabilities of foundation models or companies like Lovable and things like that are just so improving such a rapid rate that it makes no sense to have a year-long roadmap.
zh: 现在，基础模型的能力或像 Lovable 这样的公司的能力正以如此快的速度提升，以至于制定一个为期一年的路线图毫无意义。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're just incoherent.
zh: 而且它们是支离破碎的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are things that were impossible to do in November that are actually pretty easy to do right now in March.
zh: 有些事情在 11 月是不可能做到的，但在 3 月现在实际上很容易做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think you need to build an organization that's incredibly adept at, people say nimble and all that stuff, but incredibly adept at changing the roadmap almost on the fly.
zh: 所以我认为你需要建立一个极其擅长的组织，人们说敏捷等等，但极其擅长几乎在即时改变路线图。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think intermediaries like conventional PMs don't make a lot of sense versus being prepared intellectually, embracing and exploding, noticing.
zh: 我认为像传统 PM 这样的中间层意义不大，相比之下，在智力上做好准备，拥抱和爆发，发现。

speaker: Unknown
en: So someone needs to notice that, "Oh, wow, we can actually do this."
zh: 所以有人需要注意到，“哦，哇，我们实际上可以做这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But then exploiting it this week is the future of a high growth stellar startup.
zh: 然后在本周利用它，是高增长的杰出初创公司的未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll notice that something's now possible this week and create new features and new value for customers next week.
zh: 我们会注意到某事本周成为可能，并在下周为客户创造新功能和新价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's such an interesting area of discussion.
zh: 这是一个非常有趣的讨论领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people listening to this RPM.
zh: 很多收听这个 RPM 的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I want to try to defend that role just to see if I can convince you otherwise.
zh: 所以我想试着为那个角色辩护一下,看看能不能说服你改变想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the way, I will say Mark Andreessen had this really good visual of just what's happening here is all those three functions, it's like the standoff where they're all like, "I'm good taking...
zh: 顺便说一句,我会说 Marc Andreessen 有一个非常直观的看法,这里发生的事情是所有这三个角色,就像那种对峙一样,他们都像是,"我很乐意接受...

speaker: Unknown
en: The future is my role.
zh: 未来是我的角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: The future is design, that's engineering is the future."
zh: 未来是设计,那是工程,工程才是未来。"

speaker: Unknown
en: So the way I see it is as AI makes it easier to build and eats the middle of the software development process, just anyone can build detailed AI, here's what I want.
zh: 所以在我看来,随着 AI 让构建变得更容易并吞噬软件开发流程的中段,任何人都可以构建详细的 AI,这就是我想要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The hard part, the gap at least for now is figuring out what to build and then aligning everyone around what to build.
zh: 困难的部分,至少目前的差距是弄清楚要构建什么,然后让所有人在构建什么上达成一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: I agree with that.
zh: 我同意那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I actually think whether you talk about someone who used to be a PM or someone who used to be a designer or called an engineer, the skill is more like being a CEO now, which is what are we building and why?
zh: 我实际上认为无论你谈论的是一个以前是 PM 的人还是一个以前是设计师或被称为工程师的人,这种技能现在更像是一个 CEO,也就是我们在构建什么以及为什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: And to be a successful engineer, that trait's critical, to be a successful designer.
zh: 而且要成为一名成功的工程师,这种特质至关重要,要成为一名成功的设计师。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because the tools and the ability to actually create the thing, an object, is going to be easier and easier.
zh: 因为实际创造东西,一个物体的工具和能力将变得越来越容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the art is knowing what to build.
zh: 但艺术在于知道要构建什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another competitor of mine, Alfred Lin at Sequoia likes to talk about being a chef.
zh: 我的另一个竞争对手,红杉资本的 Alfred Lin 喜欢谈论做一名主厨。

speaker: Unknown
en: When you're a chef at a prime restaurant, you're not actually cooking the dish.
zh: 当你是一家顶级餐厅的主厨时,你实际上并不是在亲自做菜。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're sampling your colleagues and editing their work a little bit, but fundamentally, it's half a commercial role.
zh: 你在品尝你同事的作品并稍微修改一下他们的工作,但从根本上说,这是一半的商业角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: Being a chef at a famous restaurant is what's our value proposition?
zh: 作为一家著名餐厅的主厨在于我们的价值主张是什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we differentiate ourselves?
zh: 我们如何让自己与众不同?

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we brand ourselves?
zh: 我们如何打造我们的品牌?

speaker: Unknown
en: What's our segment?
zh: 我们的细分市场是什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: What's our pricing, et cetera?
zh: 我们的定价等等是什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: What's our location even?
zh: 甚至我们的地点在哪里?

speaker: Unknown
en: That's what makes a famous chef.
zh: 这才是成就一名著名主厨的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not they're literally cooking the dish all the time.
zh: 不是因为他们一直亲自动手做菜。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I 100% agree.
zh: 我百分之百同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Interestingly enough, PMs are called mini CEOs often.
zh: 有趣的是,PM 经常被称为迷你 CEO。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the important thing is it's not like, what do you call this person?
zh: 我认为重要的一点是这不像,你怎么称呼这个人?

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the question is what skill will be most ... Where are human brains still going to be necessary?
zh: 我认为问题是什么技能将最... 哪里仍然需要人类大脑?

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's-
zh: 而且这是-

speaker: Unknown
en: Business acumen.
zh: 商业头脑。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's basically business acumen.
zh: 基本上就是商业头脑。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right.
zh: 对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What will help this company grow and succeed-
zh: 什么将帮助这家公司成长并成功-

speaker: Keith Rabois
en: Exactly.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... and win?
zh: ...并获胜?

speaker: Unknown
en: I understand the company's business equation, where we're trying to go and what the inputs and connection outputs are.
zh: 我理解公司的商业公式,我们要去哪里以及投入和连接的产出是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I can, on my own, create things that move the needle or potentially move the needle.
zh: 而且我可以独自创造那些能起作用或可能起作用的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very exciting because you can actually drive impact much more easily now as an individual.
zh: 这非常令人兴奋,因为你现在实际上可以更容易地作为个人产生影响力。

speaker: Unknown
en: My conclusion based on what you just shared is of the three roles, which role is best at that?
zh: 基于你刚才分享的内容,我的结论是在这三个角色中,哪个角色最擅长那个?

speaker: Unknown
en: And historically, it'd be PMs.
zh: 历史上来看,那是 PM。

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously, I think the important thing here is it's great PMs or great engineers, designers will do well.
zh: 显然,我认为这里重要的一点是优秀的 PM 或优秀的工程师,设计师会做得很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think interestingly, what you're describing to me is what it sounds like what a great PM would be really good at basically represents [inaudible 00:39:41]
zh: 但我认为有趣的是,你向我描述的听起来像是伟大的 PM 基本上非常擅长代表 [听不清 00:39:41]

speaker: Unknown
en: If they were exceptional, I think that's right.
zh: 如果他们是卓越的,我认为那是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think a lot of the best engineers I've worked with have commercial instincts like Max Levchin has this on steroids.
zh: 但我认为与我合作过的许多最优秀的工程师都有商业直觉,就像 Max Levchin 把这发挥到了极致。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jeremy Stoppelman, he's worked with me very closely at PayPal before he started Yelp and got promoted to be engineering director and vice president of PayPal, had commercial instincts back when he was an individual contributor.
zh: Jeremy Stoppelman,在他创建 Yelp 并被提升为 PayPal 的工程总监和副总裁之前,曾在 PayPal 与我非常密切地合作,当他还是个人贡献者时就拥有商业直觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think there are great engineers who are technically proficient that have always understood the business building.
zh: 所以我认为有很棒的工程师他们在技术上很精通,并且一直理解商业构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's the ultimate unicorn is an engineer that is also very business-minded.
zh: 我认为这是终极的独角兽,即也是一个非常有商业头脑的工程师。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going to put a premium.
zh: 这将会带来溢价。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think this is what ...
zh: 我认为这就是……

speaker: Unknown
en: The age of AI will put an incredible premium on that because they're not going to need a large team.
zh: AI 时代将对此给予极高的重视,因为他们不再需要一个大团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're not going to be marshaling the forces.
zh: 你将不再需要调遣兵力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another example is a good friend of mine is director of engineering at Ramp.
zh: 另一个例子是我的一个好朋友,他是 Ramp 的工程总监。

speaker: Unknown
en: He ships as much code personally.
zh: 他个人提交的代码量很大。

speaker: Unknown
en: So he has a team of about 20 people.
zh: 所以他有一个大约 20 人的团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: He personally ships as much code as he used to as an individual contributor while he's managing a team of 20 because the tools are so great and he's become a leading pioneer in the usage of AI.
zh: 在管理一个 20 人团队的同时,他个人提交的代码量和他作为独立贡献者时一样多,因为工具太棒了,而且他已成为使用 AI 的领军先驱。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's basically using AI as a second team.
zh: 他基本上就是把 AI 作为第二支团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's basically like, "Okay, you're the team manager.
zh: 他基本上就像是,"好的,你是团队经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: You do this, you do this, you do this, this, this together, check this out, blah, blah, blah."
zh: 你做这个,你做这个,你做这个,这个,这个一起做,看看这个,等等等等。"

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that is definitely the future.
zh: 我认为这绝对是未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: I 100% agree.
zh: 我百分之百同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Engineers that are very good at that are just extra valuable.
zh: 擅长这一点的工程师价值倍增。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your take on design in the future of design, the value of design?
zh: 你对未来设计的看法,设计的价值是什么?

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it's interesting.
zh: 嗯,这很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Design and code are merging.
zh: 设计和代码正在融合。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's not clear to me who triumphs of is it code becomes design?
zh: 我不清楚谁会占上风,是代码变成了设计吗?

speaker: Unknown
en: And design just translate automatically into code.
zh: 而设计只是自动转化为代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've made some investments that bet on both in some ways, but I think they're merging in a way where they're not separate fiefdoms anymore.
zh: 我在这方面做了一些押注,在某种程度上两边都有下注,但我认为它们正在融合,不再是可以分割的领地。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am so excited to tell you about this season's supporting sponsor Vanta.
zh: 我非常激动地告诉大家本季度的赞助商 Vanta。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta helps over 15,000 companies like Cursor, Ramp, Duolingo, Snowflake, and Atlassian earn and prove trust with their customers.
zh: Vanta 帮助超过 15,000 家公司,如 Cursor、Ramp、Duolingo、Snowflake 和 Atlassian,赢得并证明客户的信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Teams are building and shipping products faster than ever thanks to AI.
zh: 得益于 AI,团队构建和发布产品的速度比以往任何时候都快。

speaker: Unknown
en: But as a result, the amount of risk being introduced into your product and your business is higher than it's ever been.
zh: 但结果就是,引入到你产品和你业务中的风险量比以往任何时候都高。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every security leader that I talk to is feeling the increasing weight of protecting their organization, their business, and not to mention their customer data.
zh: 我交谈过的每一位安全领导者都感受到了保护其组织、其业务,更不用说其客户数据的日益沉重的压力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because things are moving so fast, they are constantly reacting, having to guess at priorities, and having to make do with outdated solutions.
zh: 因为事情发展得太快了,他们不断做出反应,不得不猜测优先级,不得不凑合使用过时的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta automates compliance and risk management with over 35 security and privacy frameworks, including SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA.
zh: Vanta 通过超过 35 个安全和隐私框架(包括 SOC 2、ISO 27001 和 HIPAA)自动化合规和风险管理。

speaker: Unknown
en: This helps companies get compliant, fast, and stay compliant.
zh: 这有助于公司快速合规并保持合规。

speaker: Unknown
en: More than ever before, trust has the power to make or break your business.
zh: 比以往任何时候都更甚,信任有力量成就或毁掉你的业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Learn more at vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 在 vanta.com/lenny 了解更多信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as a listener of this podcast, you get $1,000 off Vanta.
zh: 作为本播客的听众,你可以获得 Vanta 1000 美元的优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 网址是 vanta.com/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I'm seeing is something really interesting happening with design.
zh: 我看到的是设计领域发生了一些非常有趣的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the one hand, I just did some analysis on the job market for design.
zh: 一方面,我刚刚对设计就业市场做了一些分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's basically plateaued in terms of the number of open design roles over the past three years, just hasn't gone anywhere.
zh: 在过去三年中,开放设计职位的数量基本上处于平台期,没有任何增长。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's flat.
zh: 它是扁平的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had the head of Claude design, Jenny Wen, on the podcast, and she had this insight that the design process ...
zh: 我们请到了 Claude 设计负责人 Jenny Wen 上播客,她有一个见解,即设计流程……

speaker: Unknown
en: There's no time for the traditional design process.
zh: 没有时间进行传统的设计流程。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's like engineers are shipping 17 things a day.
zh: 比如工程师们每天要发布 17 个东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's no time to sit there and help mock and prototype and all these things.
zh: 没有时间坐在那里帮忙做模型、做原型以及所有这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, let me give you a couple of concrete examples.
zh: 好吧,让我给你举几个具体的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: So at Shopify, the way they develop, they've been doing this for over two years now.
zh: 在 Shopify,他们开发的方式,他们这样做已经两年多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this may seem normal, but they have not let PMs provide PowerPoint or keynote presentations on product for two years.
zh: 所以这可能看起来很正常,但他们已经有两年不让 PM 就产品提供 PowerPoint 或 Keynote 演示文稿了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every presentation on product has to be a workable demo, and they just expect the PMs to create the products.
zh: 关于产品的每一次演示都必须是一个可运行的 Demo,他们只是期望 PM 去创造产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the execs just refuse to look at static, "We're going to have this feature."
zh: 高管们只是拒绝看静态的,"我们会有这个功能"。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, I want everything working.
zh: 不,我希望一切都是能运行的。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is for two years.
zh: 这已经持续两年了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that, again, everything's just merging together.
zh: 所以我认为,再次强调,一切都只是在融合在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's so interesting.
zh: 太有意思了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would think though, because there are so many products launching every single day, there's just endless things to pay attention to, you would think design would be a differentiator more and more.
zh: 你可能会想,因为每天都有这么多产品发布,有无数事情值得关注,你会认为设计会越来越成为一个差异化因素。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do agree with that.
zh: 我确实同意这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do agree with that actually.
zh: 我实际上确实同意这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the alpha is in design just like in marketing.
zh: 我认为 alpha 在设计方面，就像在市场营销中一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not the tools, it's not the channels, not the metrics.
zh: 这不是工具，不是渠道，也不是指标。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's the storytelling.
zh: 这是讲故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's how do you cut through the clutter in the snappiest, most compelling, possible way?
zh: 这就是你要如何以最干脆、最引人注目的方式切入杂乱的信息？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's an NP problem.
zh: 这是一个 NP 问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so many different words you can use to express the same concept, but the person who can say, "This is the way to frame it," the proverbial 1,000 songs in your market, is worth all the tools in the world.
zh: 有很多不同的词你可以用来表达同一个概念，但那个能说「这是框架的构建方式」的人，也就是市场上那传说中的 1000 首歌曲，值得拥有世界上所有的工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's the other part of my insight recently is just as AI makes it easier to build, it's kind of expanding from the middle out.
zh: 所以这也是我最近领悟的另一部分，正如 AI 让构建变得更容易，它正在从中间向外扩展。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what remains is figuring out what to build and making iterating on the idea.
zh: 而剩下的是弄清楚要构建什么，并迭代这个想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it's at the other end of the spectrum, which is distribution, getting anyone to pay any attention to what the heck you've done, because again, there's so much happening every single day.
zh: 然后在谱系的另一端，也就是分发，如何让任何人关注你到底做了什么，因为同样，每天发生的事情太多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Cutting through the clutter.
zh: 切入杂乱的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, that's always been critical.
zh: 我的意思是，这一直都很关键。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, when people are pitching me as an investor, it's one of the things I'm dialing into immediately is how the hell is this going to cut through the clutter?
zh: 我的意思是，当人们向我推销时，作为投资者，我立即关注的事情之一就是，这到底要如何切入杂乱的信息？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's one of the reasons why I don't like to take customer feedback into account because by definition, when you put something in front of a customer, that's not a proxy for the real world.
zh: 这是我不喜欢将客户反馈纳入考量的原因之一，因为根据定义，当你把东西放在客户面前时，那并不是现实世界的代理。

speaker: Unknown
en: In the real world, you have to cut through the clutter while they're going to their Barry's bootcamp, while they're doing their job, while they're raising their kids, et cetera, et cetera, while they're walking, while they're on the subway.
zh: 在现实世界中，你必须在杂乱的信息中脱颖而出，而他们要去 Barry 的训练营，他们要工作，他们要带孩子等等等等，在他们走路的时候，在乘地铁的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: An isolated fake experiment doesn't give you actionable insights and often is directionally wrong.
zh: 一个孤立的假实验无法给你可操作的洞察，而且往往是方向性错误的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Interestingly on that line, there's a few companies that are launching, Simile is one, that is simulating humans.
zh: 有趣的是在这条线上，有几家公司正在推出，Simile 是其中一家，正在模拟人类。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if you heard about this company.
zh: 我不知道你是否听说过这家公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: They basically are building AI models of the actual people so that you can simulate your marketing launch and your product experience with people before you launch.
zh: 他们基本上是在构建真实人群的 AI 模型，这样你就可以在发布之前，用人群模拟你的营销发布和产品体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if I've seen that specific company.
zh: 我不知道我是否看过那家特定的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have seen one or two pitches.
zh: 我看过一两次推介。

speaker: Unknown
en: My general question as a refrain on those type of companies is what are they training on?
zh: 我对这类公司的一般问题作为一个反复出现的问题是，他们在训练什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because again, if they're not training on the right data, it's dangerous to say you're simulating humans.
zh: 因为同样，如果他们没有在正确的数据上训练，说你正在模拟人类是很危险的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is the question.
zh: 这就是问题所在。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, man.
zh: 噢，天哪。

speaker: Unknown
en: What a weird world.
zh: 多奇怪的世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: Along these lines, actually, you have this, I don't know if you call it a hot take that AI content is going to surpass human content and that's just the future.
zh: 实际上沿着这些思路，你有这个，我不知道你是否称之为激进观点，即 AI 内容将超越人类内容，这就是未来。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, that's inevitable.
zh: 噢，那是不可避免的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was talking to someone and then she was saying in the Chinese TikTok app, it's just all AI videos now and it's very good.
zh: 我正在和某人交谈，然后她在中国版 TikTok 应用里说，现在全是 AI 视频，而且非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: She actually enjoys watching these videos and these stories.
zh: 她实际上很享受看这些视频和故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there'd be a binary sort maybe.
zh: 我认为可能会有一种二元分类。

speaker: Unknown
en: I could see products and content thriving that is clearly still human-generated and that there's some desire for authenticity.
zh: 我可以看到那些明显仍然是人类生成的产品和内容会蓬勃发展，并且对真实性有一些渴望。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like, for example, this piece of art is a Warhol.
zh: 就像，例如，这件艺术品是 Warhol 的。

speaker: Unknown
