en: Anybody could create this now, high fidelity, but there's still an appreciation for this was created by Andy Warhol.
zh: 现在任何人都可以创作这个，高保真的，但对于这是由 Andy Warhol 创作的，仍然有一种欣赏。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there's going to be a curated experience with a premium of provenance that you know is human-created.
zh: 我认为将会有一种策展体验，带有来源证明的溢价，你知道是人类创造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's just going to be a rank filtering of what's the best content and whether it's AI-generated or not.
zh: 然后将会有一种排名筛选，即什么是最好的内容，无论它是否是 AI 生成的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a little bit like the Ben Thompson strategy thing of curation versus algorithm.
zh: 这有点像 Ben Thompson 的策略东西，策展与算法。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's going to be two polls and the curation may be for human created stuff.
zh: 将会有两极，策展可能是针对人类创作的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's going to be the algorithm, which is just what's the best?
zh: 然后将有算法，也就是什么是最好的？

speaker: Unknown
en: I 100% believe that.
zh: 我 100% 相信这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: That makes so much sense.
zh: 这非常有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was just thinking the other day, it's so interesting that AI is getting very good at video.
zh: 我前几天只是在想，AI 在视频方面变得非常好，这很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Videos are actually fun to watch that are AI-generated.
zh: 看起来 AI 生成的视频实际上很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then images are getting really good, but writing is still really bad that is AI-generated.
zh: 然后图像变得真的很好，但写作仍然是 AI 生成的，真的很糟糕。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's ironic that it's called a large language model.
zh: 具有讽刺意味的是，它被称为大语言模型。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's all context, text, text trained.
zh: 它全是上下文，文本，文本训练的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's just like that's the thing it's least good at, which is really weird.
zh: 而那正是它最不擅长的事情，这真的很奇怪。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think part of that's an economic decision of token rationing by the LLMs.
zh: 嗯，我认为部分原因在于 LLM 出于经济考量所做的 token 配给。

speaker: Unknown
en: They basically are, when you prompt, they're trying to make their economics work within a bell curve distribution.
zh: 基本上，当你进行提示时，它们试图在钟形曲线分布内使其经济模式成立。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so for example, if you prompt an LLM to write something that's very short, the quality is significantly better than if it's a page to chapter to book.
zh: 举个例子，如果你提示 LLM 写一些非常短的东西，质量会比写一页、一章或一本书要好得多。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's tokening ratioing versus actual quality.
zh: 我认为这是 token 配比与实际质量的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would think though, I don't know if it was Hemmingway of just like, "If I had more time, I would've made it shorter."
zh: 不过你会这么想，我不知道那是 Hemmingway 还是就像，“如果我有更多时间，我会把它写得更短。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's more work.
zh: 这是更多的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But then you have ...
zh: 但接着你有……

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the writing, right now what works best is short, many short examples and then-
zh: 我认为写作，目前最有效的方法是简短、许多简短的例子，然后……

speaker: Unknown
en: Tweets, basically.
zh: 基本上就像推文。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... picking, editing, reprompting.
zh: ……挑选、编辑、重新提示。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a little bit like when I used to be a litigator 25 plus years ago, the hardest part of writing a brief or all the art and all the magic was the first paragraph.
zh: 这有点像我 25 年多以前做诉讼律师的时候，撰写案情简报最难的部分，所有的艺术和魔法都在第一段里。

speaker: Unknown
en: If I could write that first paragraph really well, the chance I would win the case and convince the judge would go through the roof.
zh: 如果我能把第一段写得非常好，我赢下官司并说服法官的概率就会大幅上升。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I would actually do is I might have three weeks to write a brief.
zh: 所以我实际上会这么做，我可能有三周的时间来写简报。

speaker: Unknown
en: I might spend the first week or more walking around the office, thinking through, "How am I going to nail the first three sentences?"
zh: 我可能会花第一周甚至更长的时间在办公室里走来走去，思考：“我要如何搞定前三句话？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Once I figured out how I wanted to frame those first three sentences, I could write 30 pages in two days, but getting that right could take a week or two.
zh: 一旦我想清楚了如何构建那前三句话，我可以在两天内写出 30 页，但把那部分写对可能需要一两周。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it would occur to me in the shower or literally the shower or in the middle of a run.
zh: 有时我会是在淋浴时想到的，或者真的是淋浴时，或者是在跑步的中间。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, "Oh my God, here's how I distill it."
zh: 就像是，“天哪，这就是我提炼它的方式。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Then I could just sit down and power through the rest of the brief.
zh: 然后我就可以坐下来，一口气写完简报的其余部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's a little bit like that.
zh: 我认为这有点像那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: The powering through the rest of the brief works well, you still need the first three sentences.
zh: 一口气写完简报的其余部分效果很好，你仍然需要前三句话。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there a story from that time in your life that would be interesting to share?
zh: 你人生那段时期有没有什么有趣的故事可以分享？

speaker: Unknown
en: I didn't even know about that.
zh: 我甚至都不知道那件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, not everybody knows.
zh: 所以是的，不是每个人都知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I spent the first four and a half years of my professional career as a law clerk and then litigator, which occasionally comes in handy truthfully.
zh: 我职业生涯的前四年半是作为法官助理然后是诉讼律师度过的，说实话偶尔会派上用场。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes trading off business risk against legal risk is a valuable thing to do.
zh: 有时权衡商业风险和法律风险是一件值得做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it's not accidental that many of my best investments are in financial services in heavily regulated areas because I think I can sort of do the legal risk assessment in my own brain.
zh: 有时我许多最好的投资都在金融服务和受监管严格的领域并非偶然，因为我觉得我可以在自己的脑子里做法律风险评估。

speaker: Unknown
en: This was definitely true back in the day when I invested in YouTube.
zh: 当我投资 YouTube 时，这绝对是正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think most things that lawyers learn are very inconsistent with being entrepreneur.
zh: 但我认为律师学到的大多数事情与成为创业者是非常不一致的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when you're graded as a lawyer, you learn every exam in law school virtually is issue spotting.
zh: 所以当你作为一名律师被评分时，你学到法学院几乎每场考试都是问题识别。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you get credit for identify ...
zh: 所以你会因为识别……而获得学分。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's fact patterns and identify all the issues and then resolve them.
zh: 存在事实模式，识别所有问题，然后解决它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you learn to identify everything that can go wrong, moderately useful, but very not useful as an entrepreneur.
zh: 所以你学会了识别所有可能出错的地方， moderately useful，但作为创业者非常没有用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, sure, you can identify all the reasons a company could fail, not that difficult to do.
zh: 我的意思是，当然，你可以识别公司失败的所有原因，这并不难做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: The art is solving those.
zh: 艺术在于解决这些问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: So not the best training.
zh: 所以这不是最好的训练。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you also measure your productivity by hours worked.
zh: 然后你还用工作时间来衡量你的生产力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Literally, you bill per hour.
zh: 字面上，你按小时计费。

speaker: Unknown
en: It took me two years, maybe a year and a half after converting to technology to stop tracking all my time.
zh: 我花了两年时间，也许是一年半，在转型做技术之后才停止追踪我所有的时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: I used to literally write down in my notebook, "Oh, half hour of this meeting, 20 minutes on this, blah, blah, blah."
zh: 我以前真的会在我的笔记本上写下来，“哦，这个会议半小时，这个 20 分钟，等等等等。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I just couldn't get that out of my brain.
zh: 我就是无法把那件事从脑子里赶出去。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is so funny.
zh: 太好笑了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And David Sachs was a lawyer too.
zh: David Sachs 也是个律师。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting.
zh: 很有意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's true.
zh: 没错。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter was a lawyer.
zh: Peter 是个律师。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter too.
zh: Peter 也是。

speaker: Unknown
en: Peter was smart enough to quit after five months and four days, I think.
zh: Peter 足够聪明，在五个月零四天后就辞职了，我想。

speaker: Unknown
en: David never practiced so maybe even smarter than both of us.
zh: David 从未执业过，所以可能比我们两个都更聪明。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to hit on some hot takes, contrarian takes that you have.
zh: 我要谈谈你的一些热门观点、一些你持有的反主流观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have a number of these.
zh: 你有很多这样的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I heard you share on a podcast recently.
zh: 我最近听到你在一次播客上分享过。

speaker: Unknown
en: They asked you, "Why don't you have as many contrarian takes these days?"
zh: 他们问你，“为什么你现在没有那么多反主流观点了？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're like, "Well, they've just all been proven right."
zh: 然后你说，“嗯，它们都只是被证明是对的而已。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's the problem is if you have good ideas, eventually you want them to be adopted.
zh: 问题是，如果你有好主意，最终你希望它们被采纳。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a little bit like when you're an investor, you want to be contrarian.
zh: 这有点像当你是个投资者时，你想要反主流。

speaker: Unknown
en: So Airbnb, you start with a contrarian take, but eventually if the company's going to succeed, it has to become consensus.
zh: 所以对于 Airbnb，你从一个反主流观点开始，但如果公司要成功，它最终必须成为共识。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everybody in the world has to use it and believe in it and trust it.
zh: 世界上每个人都必须使用它、相信它并信任它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you want that inflection.
zh: 所以你想要那个拐点。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't want to just have contrarian takes and then nobody believe it.
zh: 你不想只有反主流观点，然后没人相信它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you do need a refresh rate.
zh: 所以你确实需要一个刷新率。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the question is, how do you find new ideas?
zh: 然后问题是，你如何找到新主意？

speaker: Unknown
en: But you want to actually exhaust them.
zh: 但你实际上想要耗尽它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one that I think people still just would disagree with is that your advice is for, unless you're building an enterprise company, you don't actually want to be talking to customers.
zh: 所以我认为人们仍然会不同意的一个观点是，你的建议是，除非你在建立一家企业级公司，否则你其实并不想和客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I hate talking to customers.
zh: 是的，我讨厌和客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: I refuse to allow colleagues about and talk to customers.
zh: 我拒绝允许同事们去和客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can talk with the famous stuff and the Steve Jobs, the horses and the faster horses and all this stuff.
zh: 你可以谈论那些著名的东西和 Steve Jobs、马匹和更快的马以及所有这些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think it's more important is it's often directionally wrong.
zh: 但我认为更重要的是，它通常是方向错误的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Customers don't know what they want and they're very bad because it's a subconscious decision, especially for consumers.
zh: 客户不知道他们想要什么，而且他们很糟糕，因为这是一个潜意识的决定，尤其是对于消费者。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I purchase, what I wear is not a conscious decision.
zh: 我购买什么，我穿什么，不是一个有意识的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you're consciously trying to answer a subconscious decision, you actually give misleading information even when you're trying.
zh: 当你有意识地试图回答一个潜意识的决定时，你实际上会提供误导性的信息，即使你在努力。

speaker: Unknown
en: The proverbial example I like to use, but it's instructive is ask anybody who drives a super fancy car like a Porsche or a Lamborghini, why they bought the car.
zh: 我喜欢使用的那个谚语般的例子，但它很有启发性，是去问任何驾驶像保时捷或兰博基尼这样超级豪车的人，他们为什么买这辆车。

speaker: Unknown
en: 99% of the time they will tell you every reason except the real reason.
zh: 99% 的时间他们会告诉你除了真实原因之外的每一个原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: Once you realize that, you're like, "I'm never asking customers anything."
zh: 一旦你意识到这一点，你就会想，“我再也不问客户任何问题了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, a hardcore enterprise, customer development does work because there is a decision maker.
zh: 现在，对于一个硬核的企业级产品，客户开发确实有效，因为有一个决策者。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the decision maker is mostly making utilitarian decision.
zh: 而且决策者主要是在做功利主义的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: And yes, there's political forces within the organization and you may or may not be able to tap into those, but fundamentally, extracting that information is valuable, but a consumer, SMB, micro-emergent product, unmitigated disaster.
zh: 是的，组织内部有政治力量，你可能也可能无法利用那些力量，但根本上，提取那些信息是有价值的，但对于消费者、SMB、微型新兴产品，是一场毫无节制的灾难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the implication here is you need to rely on your instincts and gut and experience.
zh: 所以这里的含义是你需要依靠你的直觉、本能和经验。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, humans are humans.
zh: 我的意思是，人就是人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have this other line I like, which is everything important you need to learn about humans was written by Shakespeare.
zh: 我有另一句我喜欢的话，就是你需要了解的关于人类的所有重要事情都由莎士比亚写过了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just read Shakespeare.
zh: 读莎士比亚就行了。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's better than all the customer research.
zh: 那比所有的客户研究都好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now you are producing a movie and ultimately, this movie doesn't just need to be critically acclaimed.
zh: 现在你在制作一部电影，最终，这部电影不仅仅需要获得评论界的赞誉。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to sell tickets.
zh: 你必须卖出门票。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you're not selling tickets, you have to question, "Okay, is the trailer wrong?
zh: 所以如果你没卖出门票，你必须质疑，“好的，是预告片错了吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is our distribution where we're trying to meet people to let them know about the movie, is that wrong?"
zh: 是我们的分发渠道错了吗，我们试图去接触人们以让他们知道这部电影的地方，错了吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Fortunately, unlike a movie, you can go back and say, "Have I casted this somewhat incorrectly?
zh: 幸运的是，不像电影，你可以回去并说，“我是不是选角选得有点不对？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is the script slightly off," et cetera?
zh: 是不是剧本稍微有点偏差，”等等？

speaker: Unknown
en: But the goal is selling tickets and that's what you want to optimize for.
zh: 但目标是卖出门票，而这就是你想要优化的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you don't sell tickets successfully, economically, efficiently, you definitely want to go back in a loop and try to reorient things so that you are selling tickets.
zh: 但如果你没有成功地在经济上、高效地卖出门票，你肯定想回到一个循环中并尝试重新调整方向，这样你就能卖出门票。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this is like CAC LTB kind of stuff is what I'm hearing here.
zh: 所以这就像是 CAC LTV 这类的东西，这就是我在这里听到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so your insight here is just, it's not going to help you.
zh: 所以你这里的见解就是，它帮不了你。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're saying moreso it's actually harmful.
zh: 你在说，更多的是，它实际上是有害的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's harmful.
zh: 它是有害的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then people will say, "Yeah, I've sat in so many meetings," and this would infuriate me, but where people will be like, "I talked to eight customers, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, and I know that this isn't statistically represented," but then they pontificate for an hour.
zh: 然后人们会说，“是啊，我参加过那么多会议，”这会让我愤怒，但在那里人们会说，“我和八个客户谈过，等等等等等等，我知道这在统计上没有代表性，”但他们然后会高谈阔论一个小时。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they're like, "Oh, I know this is not blah, blah, blah."
zh: 然后他们会说，“噢，我知道这不是等等等等等等。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But once you hear this stuff, it's like we can't take this out of your brain.
zh: 但一旦你听到这些东西，这就好像我们无法把它从你的脑子里拿出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then every other subsequent meeting is like this stuff is just locked in the customer's brain.
zh: 然后接下来的每一次会议就好像这些东西就是锁在客户的脑子里一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yes, in the enterprise when you have ...
zh: 所以是的，在企业里当你有……

speaker: Unknown
en: I work with a company in AI that has 30 must win accounts.
zh: 我在人工智能领域与一家公司合作，该公司有 30 个必须拿下的客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the goal for the company over the next few years, make sure all 30, and we're doing really well, get all 30 using our product.
zh: 这是该公司未来几年的目标，确保所有 30 个，而且我们做得很好，让所有 30 个都使用我们的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Great.
zh: 很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: We actually can talk to all 30 customers and we can actually meet the decision maker, all 30 customers.
zh: 我们实际上可以与所有 30 个客户交谈，我们实际上可以见到决策者，所有 30 个客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we can influence the CEO at all 30 customers.
zh: 我们可以影响所有 30 个客户的 CEO。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is a useful exercise.
zh: 这是一个有用的练习。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're targeting a billion people on the planet, you are not getting representative feedback.
zh: 如果你的目标是地球上的 10 亿人，你不会得到有代表性的反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: Being a contrarian take, many people would not believe this as good advice.
zh: 作为一个反向观点，许多人不会相信这是好的建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have a story, maybe an example of just, wow, someone talking to a customer, saying, "We're going in the wrong direction for a while"?
zh: 你有没有故事，也许只是一个例子，哇，某人在和客户交谈，说，“我们有一段时间走错了方向”？

speaker: Unknown
en: Really-
zh: 真的——

speaker: Unknown
en: All the time.
zh: 一直都有。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're called failed companies.
zh: 它们被称为失败的公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a reason why ...
zh: 这是有原因的……

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, there's a Darwinistic efficiency to this too, which is just like, "Hey, there are things you can vet.
zh: 我的意思是，这里也有一种达尔文主义的效率，就像，“嘿，有些事情你可以审查。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it feasible to do X, Y, or Z?"
zh: 做 X、Y 或 Z 可行吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: So for example, let's go to DoorDash.
zh: 所以举个例子，让我们看看 DoorDash。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think customers told us that, "We want a button on our phone to click to deliver food," but you could talk to restaurants and say, "Hey, would you put this placard here so that people walking into your store know in the future that they can get delivered?"
zh: 我不认为客户告诉我们，“我们需要手机上的一个按钮来点击以配送食物，”但你可以和餐厅交谈，“嘿，你愿意把这个牌子放在这里，以便走进你店里的人知道他们将来可以获得配送吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, maybe.
zh: 是的，也许吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then could you run an experiment of how many deliveries per hour would you have to do to breakeven?
zh: 那么你能做一个实验，每小时需要多少次配送才能盈亏平衡？

speaker: Unknown
en: And is there enough density within this?
zh: 这里面有足够的密度吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: There are ways to improve the odds that you can make the business work, but I don't think launching the company saying, "Hey, we found 10 people in Palo Alto.
zh: 有办法提高你让业务运作的几率，但我不认为在创办公司时说，“嘿，我们在 Palo Alto 找到了 10 个人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you want this button on your phone?"
zh: 你想要手机上有这个按钮吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: So when Tony and Evan walked into my office originally, the epiphany I had was, well, they had a stat, which is 93% of the restaurants in the United States don't deliver.
zh: 所以当 Tony 和 Evan 最初走进我的办公室时，我的顿悟是，嗯，他们有一个统计数据，即美国 93% 的餐厅不提供配送。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was like, okay, seems like it should be a higher percentage than seven.
zh: 我想，好的，看起来比例应该高于 7%。

speaker: Unknown
en: Convinced.
zh: 被说服了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then when they were describing what they wanted to create, Andrew Mason of Groupon fame had famously said, "These phones, these devices should have two buttons.
zh: 然后当他们描述他们想要创造什么时，Groupon 的著名人物 Andrew Mason 曾说过，“这些手机，这些设备应该有两个按钮。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm bored and I'm hungry."
zh: ‘我很无聊’和‘我很饿’。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, "Oh my God, you're the I'm hungry button."
zh: 我想，“天哪，你就是‘我很饿’按钮。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then so it just clicked in my brain and then it was like, okay, now we need to make it economically possible to scalably do this.
zh: 然后它就在我的脑子里咔嚓一声，然后就像，好的，现在我们需要让它从经济上可能可扩展地做这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Good luck, guys.
zh: 祝好运，伙计们。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this advice is not just consumer.
zh: 而且这个建议不仅仅是针对消费者的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're also talking like DoorDash has SMB-ish-
zh: 你也在说像 DoorDash 有类似 SMB（中小企业）的——

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, like Square and things like that.
zh: 是的，像 Square 和类似的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Square, yeah.
zh: Square，是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Square is a good example.
zh: Square 是一个很好的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything sub mid-market, I think it's directionally dangerous.
zh: 任何低于中端市场的东西，我认为在方向上是危险的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the advice here is basically trust your insights.
zh: 这里的建议基本上是相信你的洞察。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to have the insight yourself.
zh: 你需要有洞察力你自己。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't find it [inaudible 00:57:57].
zh: 你无法发现它 [听不清 00:57:57]。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think typically the best companies-
zh: 我认为通常最好的公司——

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, interesting.
zh: 是的，有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's foundational insight.
zh: 有基础洞察。

speaker: Unknown
en: And people don't necessarily want to hear that, but there's logic, you can asset test and pressure test the logic.
zh: 人们不一定想听到那个，但有逻辑，你可以对逻辑进行资产测试和压力测试。

speaker: Unknown
en: To some extent, when Brian first pitched me on Airbnb, there was some interesting evidence he already had that this was going to be successful.
zh: 在某种程度上，当 Brian 最初向我推销 Airbnb 时，他已经有一些有趣的证据表明这将会成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: The number one, the one that stuck with me at the time was he gave me the exact number of Craigslist listings that said, "I want to rent someone's bedroom."
zh: 第一个，当时让我记住的是他给了我 Craigslist 列表的确切数量，上面写着，“我想租某人的卧室。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it was actually a reasonable number.
zh: 这实际上是一个合理的数字。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was 30 in the Bay Area.
zh: 在湾区是 30。

speaker: Unknown
en: But given that you had to literally type it in and have the epiphany yourself, I was like, "That's a lot actually.
zh: 但鉴于你必须真的把它打出来并且你自己才会有顿悟，我就想，“这其实挺多的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's probably a real market there."
zh: 那可能真的是一个市场。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And as soon as he said that, I was already leaning in.
zh: 当他一说那个，我就已经凑过去了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the time he finished his three-minute monologue, I was like, "This is the coolest thing ever.
zh: 当他结束他那三分钟的独白时，我就想，“这是有史以来最酷的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I need to invest."
zh: 我需要投资。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think Airbnb was a good example where he was solving his own problem, saw an opportunity.
zh: 我认为 Airbnb 是一个很好的例子，他在解决自己的问题，看到了机会。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wasn't talking to people, "Hey, would you do this sort of thing?"
zh: 并没有跟人们谈话，“嘿，你会做这种事吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And people would've said if he'd sampled the wrong people, definitely would've got feedback that was like, "No, very high risk."
zh: 而且如果他抽样了错误的人，人们肯定会给出像这样的反馈，“不，风险很高。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a good example where you had sampled 10 random people, good chance that nine plus percent would say, "No, I would never do that."
zh: 这是一个很好的例子，如果你抽样了 10 个随机的人，很有可能百分之九以上的人会说，“不，我绝不会那样做。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You know what's really interesting?
zh: 你知道什么真的很有趣吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I was just watching Taylor Swift's acceptance speech at this award show, iHeartMedia something or other.
zh: 我刚刚在看 Taylor Swift 在那个颁奖典礼上的获奖感言，iHeartMedia 什么的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And she gave this really powerful speech that when she was starting out, she was just kind of at home working on songs, learning piano, just in a room on her own and had thousands of hours to just iterate and learn and get better.
zh: 她发表了一个非常有力的演讲，说当她刚开始的时候，她只是在家里写歌，学钢琴，只是独自在一个房间里，有数千小时的时间去迭代、学习和变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Versus today, if you were to do this, you'd be posting it, sharing it, people giving you feedback constantly.
zh: 对比今天，如果你要这样做，你会发布它，分享它，人们不断地给你反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And her advice is just find ways to just not expose yourself to people for a long time.
zh: 她的建议就是找到方法，在很长一段时间内不要暴露自己给人们。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I have a couple of friends who are artists in the music industry, and I think what she's saying is one of the reasons why it's sometimes difficult for artists who had success to recreate the success.
zh: 所以我有几个朋友是音乐行业的艺术家，我认为她所说的是为什么那些曾经成功的艺术家有时很难重现成功的原因之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because the first success was not data-driven, was not customer feedback driven.
zh: 因为第一次成功不是数据驱动的，不是客户反馈驱动的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was like, "I'm creating this and it's resonating."
zh: 就像，“我创造了这个，而且它产生了共鸣。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Then because they have an audience, someone, either they or their manager or whoever, the label, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, wants them to get feedback.
zh: 然后因为他们有了观众，有人，无论是他们自己还是他们的经理或任何人，唱片公司，等等等等等等，希望他们获得反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it creates derivative works, not strictly legally, but derivative works, they're less inspired.
zh: 而这创造了衍生作品，不是严格法律意义上的，而是衍生作品，它们缺乏灵感。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a podcast that Jack Altman did with my friend, Alex, from The Chainsmokers.
zh: Jack Altman 和我的朋友 Alex（来自 The Chainsmokers）做了一个播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: He actually talks about this at some length that they created this song that actually didn't resonate with their normal audience, but actually resonated with a different audience, which is interesting.
zh: 他实际上详细谈论了这一点，他们创作了一首实际上并没有与他们正常的观众产生共鸣，但实际上与不同的观众产生共鸣的歌曲，这很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think you can get trapped with success.
zh: 所以我认为你可能会被成功困住。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a good illustration actually.
zh: 这实际上是一个很好的例证。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's also this concept of the ugly baby in Creativity, Inc., I don't know if you read that book, Ed Catmull, about every idea at Pixar, every great idea starts as this ugly baby that no one wants to help and pay attention to and just, "Get those out of here."
zh: 还有《Creativity, Inc.》中的“丑宝宝”这个概念，我不知道你读没读过那本书，Ed Catmull 写的，关于 Pixar 的每一个想法，每一个伟大的想法都始于这个没有人想要帮助和关注的丑宝宝，只是，“把这些弄走。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like a startup.
zh: 就像一家初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, that is actually the startup.
zh: 我的意思是，那实际上就是初创公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: I use this prism as an investor, which is when I make a seed investment or series A investment, let's say, I want half of my friends who are VCs to laugh at me.
zh: 我作为投资者使用这个棱镜，也就是当我进行种子投资或 A 轮投资时，比方说，我希望我作为 VC 的朋友有一半会嘲笑我。

speaker: Unknown
en: Literally laugh because I know most of the people I compete with pretty well.
zh: 真的笑，因为我很了解我竞争的大多数人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I'm running this algorithm through my brain.
zh: 所以我在脑子里运行这个算法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are these people going to laugh?
zh: 这些人会笑吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: If so, this is potentially a great investment because it's an ugly baby.
zh: 如果是，这可能是很棒的投资，因为它是个丑宝宝。

speaker: Unknown
en: And ugly babies are the ones where there's real alpha.
zh: 而丑宝宝才是有真正 alpha 的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's so interesting you say that.
zh: 你这么说真有意思。

speaker: Unknown
en: I did some research recently on what are signs.
zh: 我最近做了一些关于什么是迹象的研究。

speaker: Unknown
en: We interviewed, this is me and Terrence Rohan.
zh: 我们采访了，这是我和 Terrence Rohan。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if you know him, VC.
zh: 我不知道你认不认识他，VC。

speaker: Unknown
en: We interviewed early employee, people that have joined early generational companies many times.
zh: 我们采访了早期员工，那些多次加入早期世代公司的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They joined OpenAI early before anyone knew about it.
zh: 他们在任何人都知道之前很早就加入了 OpenAI。

speaker: Unknown
en: Palantir, really early, Stripe.
zh: Palantir，非常早，Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we asked them, "What did you look for?"
zh: 所以我们问他们，“你们当时在寻找什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's three patterns.
zh: 有三个模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of them was exactly that.
zh: 其中之一正是那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: The idea, people laughed at them.
zh: 那个想法，人们嘲笑他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: They thought it was crazy.
zh: 他们认为这很疯狂。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is never going to work.
zh: 这永远不会成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: Palantir, for example, OpenAI, for example.
zh: 例如 Palantir，例如 OpenAI。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: My parents used to laugh at almost all my jobs in tech.
zh: 我的父母过去几乎嘲笑我在科技行业的所有工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It used to be very funny.
zh: 这过去很好笑。

speaker: Unknown
en: They thought I was going to be homeless because most of them did not make sense to them.
zh: 他们以为我会无家可归，因为他们大多数人觉得这毫无意义。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's classic parents.
zh: 那是典型的父母。

speaker: Unknown
en: No idea what the hell people do in tech.
zh: 根本不知道人们在这个技术行业到底在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Keith Rabois
en: They did appreciate Stripe though. My mom always appreciated Stripe.
zh: 不过他们确实很欣赏 Stripe。我妈妈一直很欣赏 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: That makes sense.
zh: 这有道理。

speaker: Unknown
en: And always tried to lobby me to invest, and I finally listened.
zh: 并且总是试图游说我去投资，而我终于听了进去。

speaker: Unknown
en: Nice.
zh: 不错。

speaker: Unknown
en: Good job, Mom.
zh: 干得好，妈妈。

speaker: Unknown
en: She earned her keep.
zh: 她赢得了她的地位。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We just shifted perspective because Keith's iPad was dying.
zh: 我们刚刚转移了视角，因为 Keith 的 iPad 快没电了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Classic, that's maybe the downside of the iPad [inaudible 01:02:41]
zh: 典型，这也许是 iPad 的缺点 [听不清 01:02:41]

speaker: Unknown
en: The downside of the iPad is I use it too much.
zh: iPad 的缺点是我用得太多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's product market fit.
zh: 这就是产品市场契合。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to actually follow up on this discussion we're having about just finding great companies.
zh: 我想接着跟进我们刚才关于寻找优秀公司的讨论。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people are starting AI companies now.
zh: 现在很多人在创办 AI 公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's so many launching.
zh: 有太多公司正在发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: As an investor, I'm just curious, what's a sign that this is a worthwhile idea considering the endless number of startups launching?
zh: 作为投资者，我很好奇，考虑到发布的初创公司数量无穷无尽，什么迹象表明这是一个值得做的想法？

speaker: Unknown
en: And maybe what are some just flags that are like, "Okay, maybe don't work on that idea"?
zh: 也许有哪些警示信号是像，“好吧，也许不要在这个想法上工作了”？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, the existential question that everybody talks about these days is are the foundation labs just going to be so proficient that there's no oxygen?
zh: 嗯，如今每个人都在谈论的存在主义问题是，基础实验室会不会变得如此精通以至于没有生存空间？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because if you're building a successful startup, you need to build for eight to 20 years into the future.
zh: 因为如果你在构建一家成功的初创公司，你需要为未来 8 到 20 年做构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: Whether you just discount a cap flow analysis or some other prism, it doesn't matter.
zh: 无论你是做现金流折现分析还是通过其他视角，这都不重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: Ultimately, you have to build for something that's durable for decades.
zh: 最终，你必须构建某种能持续几十年的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the rate of progress by the foundational labs, not just one, multiple of them, really does start creating questions about the sustainability of even companies that look like they're thriving in the short term.
zh: 而基础实验室的进步速度，不仅仅是一家，是多家，确实开始让人质疑那些短期内看似蓬勃发展的公司的可持续性。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's one question.
zh: 所以这是一个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: The second question I'd ask is very typical.
zh: 我要问的第二个问题非常典型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've always asked this question for 25 years, which are, what are the accumulating advantages of this startup?
zh: 25 年来我一直问这个问题，那就是，这家初创公司的累积优势是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: You do want to believe that over time you create an unfair advantage.
zh: 你确实要相信随着时间的推移你会创造一种不公平的优势。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there are different species of accumulating advantages.
zh: 累积优势有不同的种类。

speaker: Unknown
en: The one that people immediately gravitate to, but it's only one illustration of a set of options is network effects.
zh: 人们立刻倾向于的那种，但这只是一组选项中的一个例子，是网络效应。

speaker: Unknown
en: But you want something that, over time, makes the business better and better and better, arguably easier and easier at some point.
zh: 但你想要某种随着时间的推移让业务变得越来越好、越来越好，可以说在某些时候越来越容易的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you see those sorts of things at the beginning when you're seeing a seed stage startup?
zh: 当你在看种子阶段初创公司时，你在开始阶段能看到这些事情吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, it's a great question because I think people can conflate two things.
zh: 是的，这是个好问题，因为我认为人们会把两件事混为一谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a difference between seeing it and understanding the potential.
zh: 看到它和理解潜力是有区别的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I'm looking for when I need a founder is can they articulate where the accumulating advantages can be in theory conceptually.
zh: 所以当我在寻找创始人时，我在寻找的是他们能否在理论上和概念上阐明累积优势可能在哪里。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't have to have demonstrated it.
zh: 他们不需要证明它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes, there's an occasional example.
zh: 是的，偶尔会有例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Once every five years you might find one where early you can actually point your finger on it empirically, but that's way too strict as a bar for an early stage investor.
zh: 每五年你可能会发现一个早期的例子，你实际上可以在经验上指出它，但这对于早期投资者来说门槛太严格了。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I personally want the founder to articulate to me where they can build accumulating advantages and maybe even sequentially identify when they would start either taking advantage of them, leveraging them or measuring them.
zh: 但我个人希望创始人向我阐明他们可以在哪里构建累积优势，甚至可能按顺序确定他们何时会开始利用它们、利用它们或衡量它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when you're evaluating startups these days, I know this is a very hard question to answer, but just is there anything in particular you're looking for that you get really excited about?
zh: 所以当你如今评估初创公司时，我知道这是一个很难回答的问题，但有什么特别的东西是你寻找并且让你感到兴奋的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm a founder-driven investor.
zh: 我是一个由创始人驱动的投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the only thing I really care about is, does this founder have a non-zero chance of changing an industry or the world?
zh: 所以我唯一关心的是，这位创始人是否有改变一个行业或世界的非零概率？

speaker: Unknown
en: And if they do for a seed or series A investment, I'm in, period.
zh: 如果对于种子轮或 A 轮投资他们有的话，我就加入，就这样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't ask any other questions.
zh: 别问任何其他问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's all I need to identify.
zh: 这就是我需要识别的全部。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not every investor who's been successful has the same algorithm they're running.
zh: 并不是每一位成功的投资者都在运行相同的算法。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are technology-driven investors.
zh: 有技术驱动的投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would say Mark Andreessen's probably someone like that.
zh: 我会说 Marc Andreessen 大概就是那种人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vinod's a mix.
zh: Vinod 是个混合体。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's both founder-driven and a technology-driven investor.
zh: 他既是创始人驱动也是技术驱动的投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are investors who are products/market-driven investors.
zh: 有些投资者是产品/市场驱动的投资者。

speaker: Unknown
en: My colleague, David Wyden, I'd say is that.
zh: 我的同事 David Wyden，我想就是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think Alfred Lin at Sequoia is mostly that.
zh: 我觉得红杉的 Alfred Lin 大部分时候是那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you can have different approaches, mental models, paradigms.
zh: 所以你可以有不同的方法、心智模型、范式。

speaker: Unknown
en: But for me, it's, is this founder extraordinary?
zh: 但对我来说，是这位创始人卓越吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do I have reason to believe that this founder is the next Brian Chesky?
zh: 我有理由相信这位创始人是下一个 Brian Chesky 吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: You mentioned that you're an investor in all these companies you listed are doing incredibly well.
zh: 你提到你是你列出的所有这些公司的投资者，这些公司做得非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you're on the boards of a lot of really successful, incredibly good teams and companies.
zh: 而且你身处许多真正成功、极其优秀的团队和公司的董事会中。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there just something they are doing the way they operate that is different from companies that are not as successful?
zh: 是否只是他们做的事情，他们的运营方式，与不那么成功的公司不同？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the subtle signal, let's say very early, is speed.
zh: 我认为微妙的信号，让我们说非常早期，是速度。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's one of those things that's easy to say, but let me try to be more concrete.
zh: 这是那些容易说的事情之一，但让我试着更具体一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a tempo, an operating tempo that a successful company develops that develops very early in a company's trajectory and is incredibly impressive.
zh: 有一种节奏，一种成功的公司发展出的运营节奏，这种节奏在公司轨迹的早期就发展出来了，且令人印象深刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: I remember when Roelof Botha was on my board at Square.
zh: 我记得当 Roelof Botha 在我在 Square 的董事会时。

speaker: Unknown
en: He led the series B.
zh: 他领投了 B 轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: So he joined our board.
zh: 所以他加入了我们的董事会。

speaker: Unknown
en: And six months in, so two board meetings in, he said to me, he's like, "I haven't seen this kind of tempo since our PayPal days."
zh: 而在六个月时，也就是开了两次董事会会议后，他对我说，他就像，“自我们的 PayPal 时代以来，我还没见过这种节奏。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he'd been a VC at that point for nine years.
zh: 而在那时他已经做 VC 九年了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was curious, I said, "What are you noticing?"
zh: 我很好奇，我说，“你注意到了什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And he's like, "At board meeting X, you guys identify an opportunity or problem.
zh: 他就像，“在第 X 次董事会会议，你们识别出了一个机会或问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the next board meeting, you've shipped solutions, addressed it, featured just constantly, consistently."
zh: 而到了下一次董事会会议，你们已经发布了解决方案，解决了它，功能只是不断地、一致地。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's right.
zh: 我认为那是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the time between the seed and series A at Faire was pretty tight.
zh: 所以 Faire 种子轮和 A 轮之间的时间相当短。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I remember at the time my chief of staff was Delian Asparouhov.
zh: 我记得当时我的幕僚长是 Delian Asparouhov。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Delian said to me after the second fare board meeting that he shadowed me at, he said, "If there's one company in Silicon Valley that would cause me to leave being the VC, it'd be Faire."
zh: 而在 Delian 陪同我参加第二次 Faire 董事会会议后，他对我说，“如果硅谷有一家公司能让我离开 VC 岗位，那就是 Faire。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, "Interesting.
zh: 我就像，“有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why?"
zh: 为什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like, "The pace of execution."
zh: 他就像，“执行的速度。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And his answer was exactly the same as Roelof's.
zh: 而他的回答与 Roelof 的完全相同。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's like, "There is something slightly off.
zh: 他就像，“有些东西稍微有点不对。

speaker: Unknown
en: And by the next morning meeting, not only have they identified the root causes, but they've shipped and reacted and measured the impact of the solution.
zh: 而到了第二天早上的会议，他们不仅找出了根本原因，而且已经发布、反应并测量了解决方案的影响。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that compounds, that speed really does come out."
zh: 这会复利，那种速度真的会显现出来。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's just one trait, but you see it.
zh: 所以这只是其中一个特征，但你能看到它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It did lead me to, for example, preempt the series A of Ramp.
zh: 这确实导致我，例如，抢投了 Ramp 的 A 轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I led this seed in May-ish of 2019 and gave a term sheet to preempt the A in September.
zh: 所以我在 2019 年 5 月左右领投了种子轮，并在 9 月给出了条款清单来抢投 A 轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: So pretty quick.
zh: 所以相当快。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the two signals was how fast Ramp was able to be on the precipice of shipping the cards.
zh: 两个信号之一是 Ramp 能够多快地处于发布卡片的边缘。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'd been working in financial services for a long time at that point, 19 years or so.
zh: 在那时，我已经在金融服务领域工作了很长时间，大概 19 年。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's just a lot of moving pieces to ship a card.
zh: 发布一张卡有很多移动的部件。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need these program managers, you need the sponsoring bank, and you need this and this and this.
zh: 你需要这些项目经理，你需要发起银行，你需要这个和这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: It usually takes nine to 12 months.
zh: 这通常需要 9 到 12 个月。

speaker: Unknown
en: Best case, nine.
zh: 最好的情况，九个月。

speaker: Unknown
en: Ramp was on the precipice in three months.
zh: Ramp 在三个月内就处于发布边缘了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, "Oh my God, I've just never seen that velocity."
zh: 我就像，“天哪，我从未见过那种速度。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That was one of two, maybe three inputs.
zh: 这是两个，也许三个输入之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was like, yes, makes sense to double down already, even if we hadn't shipped anything yet.
zh: 我就像，是的，即使我们还没有发布任何东西，现在已经很有意义要加倍下注了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that's one.
zh: 所以我认为那是其中一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Critical density of talent, you see companies just creating an unfair advantage.
zh: 人才的关键密度，你看到公司只是创造了一种不公平的优势。

speaker: Unknown
en: The team was X when you invested in them.
zh: 当你投资他们时，这个团队是 X。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, "Wow, this team is getting better, deeper, better," et cetera.
zh: 所以，“哇，这个团队正在变得更好、更深、更好”等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's another signal.
zh: 所以这是另一个信号。

speaker: Unknown
en: The third thing that I've noticed though is I think they have a different hiring philosophy ultimately, and maybe there's exceptions to this, but most of the companies I work with that are thriving have basically skipped hiring senior people, senior experienced people, mostly internally group talent.
zh: 不过我注意到的第三件事是，我认为他们最终有一种不同的招聘理念，也许这其中有例外，但我合作的大多数蓬勃发展的公司基本上都跳过了招聘资深人士、有经验的资深人士，主要是内部群体人才。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that model has worked really well.
zh: 我认为那个模式运作得非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's definitely true of Ramp, definitely true of Trade Republic.
zh: 对于 Ramp 来说肯定是真的，对于 Trade Republic 来说肯定也是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It seems to be a mostly common ingredient, but there's probably exceptions.
zh: 这似乎是一个普遍的要素，但也可能有例外。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow, that is an incredible answer instead of traits.
zh: 哇，关于特质，这是一个令人难以置信的回答。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just to be clear, what you're saying on that third piece, so it's not hire fan CVPs from other successful companies and instead develop people internally as a trait of-
zh: 只是为了澄清，关于那第三点你所说的，所以不是从其他成功的公司聘请那些疯狂的副总裁，而是从内部培养人员作为一种特质——

speaker: Unknown
en: Internally and almost turned it into a competitive advantage, meaning your strategy.
zh: 内部培养，并且几乎将其转化为竞争优势，也就是你的策略。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're just not even going to interview people.
zh: 我们甚至不去面试任何人。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're not going to try.
zh: 我们不打算尝试。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're just going to promote from within.
zh: 我们只打算从内部提拔。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think in some roles, it's not like you're hiring a GC typically right out of law school.
zh: 我认为在某些角色中，这并不是说你通常刚从法学院毕业就聘请总法律顾问。

speaker: Unknown
en: But although we have done that once and it worked out pretty well, believe it or not, but I wouldn't recommend that.

en: One last hot take that I know you have that I want to make sure we share is this idea of criticizing in public versus in private.
zh: 我知道的你有最后一个激烈观点，我想确保我们要分享的是关于公开批评与私下批评的想法。

en: Talk about that.

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.

speaker: Unknown
en: So this is a lesson I actually absorbed from one of the great founders I work with and like many great founders, they have their own management philosophy.

speaker: Unknown

zh: 最重要的原则之一是在公开场合批评人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And when you decompose the logic of it's so obviously true, but almost no one does this and very few people talk about it even if they do it.
zh: 当你分解它的逻辑时，它是如此显而易见地正确，但几乎没有人这样做，即使他们做了，也很少有人谈论它。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you think about it, when you give people feedback negative individually, you're optimizing for the atomic unit, not the system.
zh: 所以如果你想一想，当你单独给予人们负面反馈时，你是在为原子单元优化，而不是为系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: The reason why to do it in public is it's more important for all the colleagues to understand that there's an issue, it's being addressed versus they usually have suspicions, let's say, or concerns.
zh: 公开这样做的原因是，让所有同事了解存在问题并且问题正在得到解决，这一点更为重要，而不是像他们通常那样有怀疑或顾虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you've channeled the negative feedback to the individual, they don't know that you're addressing this, that you're on top of it, you're aware you're addressing it.
zh: 而且，如果你将负面反馈传达给了个人，他们并不知道你正在解决这个问题，你正在掌控它，你意识到你正在解决它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it's a collaborative.
zh: 现在这是一种协作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then also it lets other people kind of raise their hand and say, "You know what?
zh: 然后这也让其他人有点举手说，“你知道吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I can help with that," or et cetera.
zh: 我可以帮忙，”等等。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, it becomes like a team building exercise in some way versus like, "Oh, you have this deficiency, go fix it yourself."
zh: 所以，在某种程度上，这变成了一种团队建设练习，而不是像，“噢，你有这个缺陷，自己去修复它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the rest of the company is nervous about why this problem is persisting.

speaker: Unknown
en: When people hear this, it may feel like, "Oh, wait," it feels aggressive to be criticized everyone public comment.

speaker: Unknown
en: It could be very effective too.
